subject: ObGyn
question: A 62-year-old woman presents with postmenopausal bleeding, and ultrasonography shows a unilateral solid-cystic ovarian mass with endometrial thickening. Histology from the ovarian lesion demonstrates Call-Exner bodies and nuclei with longitudinal grooves, suggesting an estrogen-secreting sex-cord stromal tumour. Which additional tissue evaluation is most important before definitive treatment is planned?
options: Directed cervical punch biopsy
Contralateral ovarian wedge biopsy
Image-guided omental core biopsy
Office endometrial biopsy
correctAnswer: Office endometrial biopsy
explanation: An adult granulosa-cell tumour may produce estrogen, exposing the endometrium to prolonged unopposed stimulation and increasing the risk of hyperplasia or endometrial carcinoma. Postmenopausal bleeding with a thickened endometrium therefore warrants endometrial sampling; the other biopsies require their own specific lesion or staging indication.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman whose anomaly scan confirms fetal anencephaly asks how the defect relates to antenatal serum screening and how to reduce recurrence in a future pregnancy. The fetal defect is open, and neural-tube closure in a subsequent embryo would occur before many women attend their first antenatal visit. Which marker-and-prevention pair is correct?
options: Raised maternal serum AFP; high-dose folic acid begun at 14 weeks
Reduced maternal serum AFP; high-dose folic acid begun before conception
Raised maternal serum AFP; high-dose folic acid begun before conception
Reduced maternal serum AFP; high-dose folic acid begun at 14 weeks
correctAnswer: Raised maternal serum AFP; high-dose folic acid begun before conception
explanation: Anencephaly is an open neural-tube defect, so fetal AFP leaks into amniotic fluid and raises maternal serum AFP. After a neural-tube-defect-affected pregnancy, high-dose folic acid should begin before conception and continue through the first trimester because starting after neural-tube closure cannot prevent the defect.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A 20-year-old has hyperemesis, uterus 16-week size at 10 weeks, theca-lutein cysts and the shown intrauterine image. TSH is suppressed, beta-hCG is very high. What is the best initial uterine treatment?
options: Hysterectomy in selected patients after counselling and stabilization
Repeat evacuation after reassessment if retained tissue is suspected
Chemotherapy only when criteria for persistent GTN are met
Suction evacuation with serial beta-hCG follow-up
correctAnswer: Suction evacuation with serial beta-hCG follow-up
explanation: Suction evacuation with serial beta-hCG follow-up is the standard management sequence for molar pregnancy when fertility preservation is relevant. Very high beta-hCG, uterus larger than dates and absent live fetus support the diagnosis. Immediate IUCD insertion and routine hysterectomy do not match the usual initial pathway.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 14-year-old with normal secondary sexual characteristics has primary amenorrhoea, monthly lower-abdominal pain and an episode of acute urinary retention. Examination reveals a tense bluish membrane bulging at the introitus, and pelvic ultrasonography shows haematocolpos without an upper genital-tract anomaly. Which procedure most directly relieves the obstruction while preserving normal anatomy?
options: Resection of a transverse vaginal septum
Excision of an obstructed rudimentary horn
Vaginoplasty for Mullerian agenesis
Cruciate hymenotomy with controlled drainage
correctAnswer: Cruciate hymenotomy with controlled drainage
explanation: A tense bluish introital membrane with cyclical pain and haematocolpos identifies an imperforate hymen. Cruciate hymenotomy permits controlled evacuation and marsupialisation of the hymenal edges; septal resection, rudimentary-horn excision and vaginoplasty treat different obstructive or developmental anomalies.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 32-year-old at 36 weeks has painful grouped genital ulcers. A smear from the lesion is shown. Which intrapartum plan is correct if lesions are active in labour?
options: Acyclovir suppression from late pregnancy to reduce recurrence risk
Neonatal evaluation when intrapartum exposure is suspected
Cesarean delivery when active genital herpes lesions are present in labour
Vaginal birth when there are no active lesions or prodromal symptoms
correctAnswer: Cesarean delivery when active genital herpes lesions are present in labour
explanation: Tzanck-type smear supports HSV; active genital lesions/prodrome at labour are an indication for cesarean delivery. HSV is suggested by painful grouped ulcers or a Tzanck-type smear. Acyclovir treats HSV; active genital lesions or prodrome during labour require cesarean delivery to reduce neonatal transmission.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A breastfeeding woman requests a copper intrauterine device after an uncomplicated delivery and has no puerperal sepsis or postpartum haemorrhage. She missed both postplacental and immediate-postpartum insertion and now asks when placement should occur, because uterine involution changes expulsion and perforation considerations. Which timing interval is generally deferred in routine postpartum practice?
options: After 48 hours but before 6 weeks postpartum
Within 10 minutes after placental delivery
From 10 minutes through 48 hours postpartum
At or after 6 weeks postpartum
correctAnswer: After 48 hours but before 6 weeks postpartum
explanation: Postplacental placement, insertion within 48 hours and interval placement at or after 6 weeks are accepted when eligibility criteria are satisfied. Routine insertion from more than 48 hours to less than 6 weeks is generally deferred because the involuting uterus has a less favourable expulsion and perforation profile.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 35-year-old gravida 4 with two previous caesarean deliveries has an anterior placenta previa overlying the uterine scar. Ultrasonography shows placental lacunae and loss of the retroplacental clear zone, raising concern for placenta accreta spectrum and major haemorrhage at delivery. Which pathologic alteration most directly permits abnormal villous adherence or invasion at this site?
options: Excessively deep physiologic remodelling of the spiral arteries
Premature decidual haemorrhage behind a normally adherent placenta
Deficient decidua basalis at the scarred implantation site
Ascending neutrophilic inflammation of chorion and amnion
correctAnswer: Deficient decidua basalis at the scarred implantation site
explanation: Placenta accreta spectrum arises where deficient decidualisation, often over a uterine scar, leaves an abnormal endometrial-myometrial interface through which chorionic villi adhere to or invade myometrium. Spiral-artery remodelling concerns placentation physiology, while retroplacental haemorrhage and chorioamnionitis cause different obstetric complications.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A cervical screening clinic is matching results with the usual next step. Match the following: Column I: 1. Visible suspicious cervical lesion 2. HSIL cytology 3. AIS on biopsy with completed family after invasion excluded 4. Hydronephrosis from cervical cancer Column II: a. Biopsy of lesion b. Colposcopy with biopsy/excision as indicated c. Simple hysterectomy d. Classic stage IIIB implication Which option gives the correct matching?
explanation: A visible lesion needs biopsy, HSIL needs colposcopic/excisional assessment, AIS after exclusion of invasion is definitively treated by hysterectomy when family is complete, and hydronephrosis is a classic IIIB feature.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A haemodynamically stable woman requests termination of a confirmed 9-week intrauterine pregnancy and has no contraindication to antiprogestin or prostaglandin therapy. Ectopic pregnancy has been excluded, counselling includes expected bleeding and follow-up, and surgical evacuation remains available if treatment fails. Which pharmacologic sequence is the standard medical regimen?
options: Mifepristone followed by misoprostol
Methotrexate followed by misoprostol
Misoprostol followed by mifepristone
Mifepristone followed by oxytocin
correctAnswer: Mifepristone followed by misoprostol
explanation: Mifepristone first blocks progesterone support and sensitises the uterus, after which misoprostol induces cervical ripening and uterine contractions. Methotrexate is not the routine regimen for an eligible intrauterine pregnancy, reversing the sequence is less effective, and oxytocin is not the standard early-pregnancy partner drug.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 43-year-old woman has persistent intermenstrual spotting despite correction of hypothyroidism and a normal cervical examination. Transvaginal ultrasonography followed by saline-infusion sonography demonstrates a focal echogenic intracavitary lesion with a feeding vessel, while the surrounding endometrium is not diffusely thickened. Which investigation provides direct diagnosis and definitive treatment in the same sitting?
options: Blind dilatation and endometrial curettage
Pipelle aspiration of the endometrial cavity
Repeat saline-infusion sonography with surveillance
Hysteroscopy with directed polypectomy
correctAnswer: Hysteroscopy with directed polypectomy
explanation: A focal lesion with a feeding vessel is typical of an endometrial polyp. Hysteroscopy visualises its attachment and permits complete directed removal for histopathology; blind curettage or Pipelle sampling can miss a focal lesion, while repeat imaging does not treat persistent symptomatic disease.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 46-year-old screening-positive woman undergoes colposcopy, and biopsy of an acetowhite cervical lesion shows invasive squamous-cell carcinoma with koilocytotic change in adjacent epithelium. The oncogenic viral protein pathway involves degradation of p53 and inactivation of retinoblastoma protein. Which high-risk HPV genotype is most strongly associated with this histologic cancer type?
options: HPV genotype 16
HPV genotype 18
HPV genotype 31
HPV genotype 45
correctAnswer: HPV genotype 16
explanation: HPV 16 is the most prevalent oncogenic genotype in cervical squamous-cell carcinoma. HPV 18 and 45 have a relatively stronger association with cervical adenocarcinoma, while HPV 31 is also high risk but contributes a smaller proportion of invasive squamous cancers than HPV 16.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with three previous caesarean deliveries has complete placenta previa and antenatal imaging strongly suggestive of placenta increta. She has completed her family, is stable, and delivery is being planned in a tertiary centre with interventional radiology, massive-transfusion capability, urology and critical-care support. Which definitive operative approach best minimises catastrophic haemorrhage from attempted placental separation?
options: Planned caesarean hysterectomy after manual placental separation
Planned caesarean delivery with routine focal myometrial resection
Planned induction of labour followed by postpartum uterine curettage
Planned caesarean hysterectomy with the placenta left in situ
correctAnswer: Planned caesarean hysterectomy with the placenta left in situ
explanation: For antenatally suspected placenta accreta spectrum when definitive surgery is appropriate, planned caesarean hysterectomy is performed without disrupting the placenta. Manual separation can trigger torrential haemorrhage; conservative focal resection is reserved for carefully selected expertise-dependent cases, and induction with curettage is unsafe for this presentation.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 45-year-old woman presents with recurrent postcoital bleeding despite a previously negative screening test. Speculum examination shows a 2-cm friable irregular cervical growth that bleeds on contact, while bimanual examination finds no obvious parametrial fixation. The team needs histological confirmation before staging imaging or definitive treatment. Which diagnostic act is most appropriate now?
options: Repeat cervical cytology with high-risk HPV testing
Endocervical curettage as the sole tissue procedure
Diagnostic cold-knife conisation before sampling the visible growth
Colposcopically directed punch biopsy of the visible cervical lesion
correctAnswer: Colposcopically directed punch biopsy of the visible cervical lesion
explanation: A visible friable cervical lesion suspicious for invasive cancer requires directed biopsy for histological confirmation, regardless of a previous screening result. Cytology and HPV testing are screening or triage tests and cannot exclude invasion in a gross lesion. Endocervical curettage alone may miss an ectocervical mass, while diagnostic conisation is unnecessary before a straightforward punch biopsy and may complicate subsequent assessment.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: During evaluation of postcoital bleeding, the clinician introduces the displayed self-retaining bivalve instrument into the vagina and opens its blades. This permits inspection of the ectocervix, application of acetic acid and collection of cervical samples before proceeding to bimanual examination. What is the instrument's principal mechanical function?
options: Separating the vaginal walls to expose the cervix
Grasping the anterior cervical lip to provide traction
Dilating the cervical canal before intrauterine entry
Measuring the depth and direction of the uterine cavity
correctAnswer: Separating the vaginal walls to expose the cervix
explanation: A bivalve vaginal speculum opens to separate the anterior and posterior vaginal walls and expose the cervix for inspection or sampling. Cervical traction requires a vulsellum or tenaculum, canal dilatation uses graduated dilators, and a uterine sound measures cavity length and direction.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A teenager has an ovarian mass. The image shows Schiller-Duval-type morphology, while beta-hCG is normal. Which tumor marker is most useful for follow-up?
options: CA-125 for epithelial ovarian tumor
Beta-hCG for choriocarcinoma pattern
Serum AFP for yolk sac tumor follow-up
LDH for dysgerminoma follow-up
correctAnswer: Serum AFP for yolk sac tumor follow-up
explanation: Serum AFP is the useful tumor marker for yolk sac tumor follow-up. Schiller-Duval morphology and raised AFP identify a malignant germ-cell tumor. CA-125, beta-hCG and LDH fit other ovarian tumor pathways.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 30-year-old woman develops secondary amenorrhoea and infertility after postpartum haemorrhage was managed with repeated uterine curettage. A progesterone-withdrawal test produces no bleeding despite adequate estrogenisation, and hysteroscopy confirms dense intrauterine adhesions with partial cavity obliteration. Which intervention is the primary anatomical treatment before fertility is reassessed?
options: Repeat blind dilatation and uterine curettage
Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis under direct vision
Hysteroscopic endometrial ablation of residual tissue
Immediate in-vitro fertilisation without cavity correction
correctAnswer: Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis under direct vision
explanation: Asherman syndrome is treated by hysteroscopic division of adhesions under direct vision to restore the uterine cavity while limiting further endometrial trauma. Blind curettage and endometrial ablation can worsen damage, and embryo transfer is inappropriate until a cavity capable of implantation has been restored and reassessed.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A fetus/newborn has the asymmetric limb abnormality shown. Karyotype is normal and no symmetric skeletal dysplasia pattern is present. Which counseling point is most accurate?
options: Congenital infection with growth restriction and calcification
Open neural-tube defect with craniospinal abnormality
Amniotic band sequence with asymmetric constriction defects
Skeletal dysplasia with symmetric limb shortening
correctAnswer: Amniotic band sequence with asymmetric constriction defects
explanation: The image shows asymmetric constriction/amputation, favoring amniotic band sequence. Amniotic band sequence is a disruptive, usually sporadic process. The key is asymmetric constriction, amputation or entrapment rather than a symmetric inherited skeletal dysplasia.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with biopsy-proven cervical carcinoma has pelvic MRI showing parametrial extension to the pelvic wall and unilateral ureteric obstruction with hydronephrosis. Cystoscopy and proctoscopy show no bladder or rectal mucosal invasion, and imaging reveals no pelvic or para-aortic nodal metastasis. Under FIGO staging, which stage is assigned by the local disease?
options: Stage IIB
Stage IIIB
Stage IIIC1
Stage IVA
correctAnswer: Stage IIIB
explanation: Tumour extension to the pelvic wall or tumour-related hydronephrosis or a non-functioning kidney defines FIGO stage IIIB. Stage IIB has parametrial invasion without pelvic-wall involvement, stage IIIC1 requires pelvic nodal metastasis, and stage IVA requires invasion of bladder or rectal mucosa.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 31-year-old woman with severe dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia and three years of infertility has a 4-cm ovarian endometrioma and bilateral distal tubal occlusion on hysterosalpingography. Semen analysis is normal, ovarian reserve remains acceptable and there is no hydrosalpinx requiring isolation, while extensive surgery could reduce oocyte yield. Which fertility strategy offers the most direct route to pregnancy?
options: In-vitro fertilisation after endometriosis and ovarian-reserve assessment
Laparoscopic endometrioma excision followed by timed intercourse
Ovulation induction followed by intrauterine insemination
Bilateral distal tubal reconstructive surgery followed by expectant conception
correctAnswer: In-vitro fertilisation after endometriosis and ovarian-reserve assessment
explanation: Bilateral distal tubal occlusion prevents natural conception and limits the value of ovulation induction or intrauterine insemination. In-vitro fertilisation bypasses the damaged tubes and is the most direct strategy after assessing ovarian reserve and endometriosis symptoms. Endometrioma surgery is individualised because it can reduce ovarian reserve, while bilateral reconstructive surgery has variable success and adds ectopic-pregnancy risk.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A 29-year-old multiparous woman has symptomatic uterine descent caused predominantly by marked infravaginal cervical elongation, with a relatively well-supported uterine body. She desires uterine preservation, has no future fertility priority and has normal cervical cytology after evaluation of contact bleeding. Which classic operation is specifically designed for this pattern of prolapse?
options: Laparoscopic sacrohysteropexy
Manchester-Fothergill operation
Le Fort partial colpocleisis
Vaginal hysterectomy with pelvic-floor repair
correctAnswer: Manchester-Fothergill operation
explanation: The Manchester-Fothergill operation combines cervical amputation with support repair and is the classic uterine-preserving procedure for prolapse dominated by cervical elongation. Sacrohysteropexy treats apical support failure, colpocleisis is obliterative, and vaginal hysterectomy does not preserve the uterus.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: Parents of a 13-year-old girl ask whether completing HPV vaccination before sexual debut means she will never require cervical screening. The clinician explains that vaccination prevents new infection with covered oncogenic types but neither treats an established infection nor covers every carcinogenic genotype. Which long-term screening advice is correct?
options: Begin annual cervical cytology after the final vaccine dose
Begin HPV testing at sexual debut and repeat it every year
Continue screening according to the age-based national programme
Defer all cervical screening unless genital symptoms develop
correctAnswer: Continue screening according to the age-based national programme
explanation: HPV vaccination markedly reduces vaccine-type infection and related precancer but does not eliminate disease caused by every oncogenic type. Vaccinated individuals should therefore enter the same age-appropriate national screening programme. Annual cytology or HPV testing from adolescence creates unnecessary testing and harm, while symptom-triggered assessment alone would miss asymptomatic precancer.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: During an assisted vaginal breech delivery, the fetal trunk and arms have delivered but flexion of the after-coming head must be maintained. The displayed forceps has long shanks and a characteristic perineal curve that permits application while the fetal body lies below the operator's hands. Which named forceps is designed for this situation?
options: Kielland forceps
Wrigley forceps
Piper forceps
Simpson forceps
correctAnswer: Piper forceps
explanation: Piper forceps are designed for the after-coming head of a breech fetus; their long shanks and perineal curve allow application beneath the delivered body while maintaining head flexion. Kielland forceps facilitate rotation, Wrigley forceps are short outlet forceps, and Simpson forceps suit a moulded cephalic head.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A nulliparous woman with chronic hypertension undergoes uterine-artery Doppler screening at 15 weeks because of a high risk of placental disease. Bilateral early-diastolic notches and persistently raised resistance suggest that the uteroplacental circulation has not become the expected low-resistance system. Which underlying placental process best explains this waveform?
options: Acute neutrophilic inflammation of chorion and amnion
Chronic lymphohistiocytic inflammation of chorionic villi
Massive perivillous fibrin deposition in the intervillous space
Incomplete trophoblast invasion and spiral-artery remodelling
correctAnswer: Incomplete trophoblast invasion and spiral-artery remodelling
explanation: Persistent high-resistance uterine flow reflects deficient extravillous trophoblast invasion and incomplete conversion of spiral arteries into low-resistance channels. This maternal vascular malperfusion pathway is linked to pre-eclampsia and fetal growth restriction; inflammatory villous or membrane lesions and fibrin deposition have different mechanisms.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A labour-room team is matching manoeuvres with the indication. Match the following: Column I: 1. McRoberts with suprapubic pressure 2. Lovset manoeuvre 3. Mauriceau-Smellie-Veit manoeuvre 4. Vacuum cup at flexion point Column II: a. Shoulder dystocia b. Extended arms in breech c. After-coming head of breech d. Vacuum-assisted delivery Which option gives the correct matching?
explanation: McRoberts/suprapubic pressure is first-line for shoulder dystocia; Lovset helps extended arms; MSV helps the after-coming head; the flexion point is used for vacuum.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: At the 22-week anomaly scan, an axial transventricular view shows mild enlargement of a fetal lateral ventricle without obvious distortion of the calvarium. The sonologist places calipers inner-to-inner across the atrium, perpendicular to its long axis, because this standard measurement determines whether ventriculomegaly is present. Which atrial width is the diagnostic threshold?
options: 8 mm or greater
12 mm or greater
10 mm or greater
15 mm or greater
correctAnswer: 10 mm or greater
explanation: Fetal ventriculomegaly is defined by a lateral-ventricular atrial diameter of at least 10 mm on a correctly obtained transventricular plane. Measurements of 10 to 12 mm are usually classified as mild, 13 to 15 mm as moderate, and more than 15 mm as severe.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman presents with vulval irritation and profuse frothy yellow-green discharge, and speculum examination shows punctate cervical erythema. Vaginal pH is elevated and a fresh saline wet mount demonstrates motile flagellated protozoa, while pregnancy testing is negative. Which treatment plan addresses both cure and reinfection risk?
options: Intravaginal metronidazole gel without treatment of sexual partners
Oral fluconazole with concurrent treatment of sexual partners
Oral metronidazole with concurrent treatment of sexual partners
Oral doxycycline without treatment of sexual partners
correctAnswer: Oral metronidazole with concurrent treatment of sexual partners
explanation: Motile trichomonads with frothy discharge and a strawberry cervix establish trichomoniasis. An effective systemic nitroimidazole regimen is required and current sexual partners should receive presumptive treatment to reduce reinfection; topical gel is inadequate for trichomoniasis, while fluconazole and doxycycline target other infections.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 48-year-old woman has irregular heavy bleeding, endometrial thickening and a unilateral ovarian mass associated with elevated inhibin. Histology from the mass shows small follicles containing eosinophilic material, and the tumour-cell nuclei have longitudinal grooves. Which microscopic description most strongly confirms the suspected estrogen-secreting tumour?
options: Schiller-Duval bodies with hyaline globules
Signet-ring cells with intracellular mucin
Psammoma bodies with papillary epithelium
Call-Exner bodies with coffee-bean nuclei
correctAnswer: Call-Exner bodies with coffee-bean nuclei
explanation: Adult granulosa-cell tumours characteristically show Call-Exner bodies and grooved coffee-bean nuclei and may secrete estrogen and inhibin. Schiller-Duval bodies indicate yolk-sac tumour, mucin-filled signet-ring cells suggest a Krukenberg tumour, and papillae with psammoma bodies favour serous neoplasia.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 38-year-old woman with obesity and longstanding PCOS reports months of irregular prolonged bleeding after years of oligomenorrhoea. Ultrasonography shows polycystic ovarian morphology and a persistently thickened endometrium, and pregnancy and cervical causes have been excluded. Which next investigation is required to assess the principal consequence of chronic unopposed estrogen exposure?
options: Repeat transvaginal sonography after observation
Saline-infusion sonography without tissue sampling
Endometrial sampling for histopathology
Diagnostic laparoscopy with ovarian biopsy
correctAnswer: Endometrial sampling for histopathology
explanation: Chronic anovulation exposes the endometrium to unopposed estrogen and increases the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma. Persistent abnormal bleeding with endometrial thickening and risk factors warrants histologic sampling; imaging can describe the cavity but cannot exclude cellular atypia, and ovarian biopsy is unnecessary for PCOS.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with HSIL cytology undergoes an adequately oriented cone biopsy, which shows disordered maturation, marked nuclear atypia and mitoses extending through the full thickness of cervical squamous epithelium. There is no desmoplastic stromal response, and the pathologist must distinguish CIN 3 from early invasive carcinoma. Which finding establishes that the lesion remains intraepithelial?
options: Full-thickness atypia with an intact epithelial basement membrane
Lower-third atypia with an intact epithelial basement membrane
Neoplastic cells crossing the basement membrane by 2 mm
Neoplastic cells crossing the basement membrane by 6 mm
correctAnswer: Full-thickness atypia with an intact epithelial basement membrane
explanation: CIN 3 comprises severe dysplasia or carcinoma in situ confined to epithelium above an intact basement membrane, commonly involving more than two thirds to full epithelial thickness. Any unequivocal breach into stroma represents invasion; lower-third atypia corresponds to CIN 1 rather than CIN 3.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: Minutes after delivery, a previously stable woman abruptly develops severe dyspnoea, cyanosis, hypotension and a generalised seizure. Soon afterward, diffuse oozing from venepuncture sites and the uterus accompanies low fibrinogen and prolonged coagulation studies, without a preceding febrile illness. Which diagnosis best unifies the cardiopulmonary collapse and consumptive coagulopathy?
options: Massive pulmonary thromboembolism
High neuraxial block
Amniotic fluid embolism
Fulminant puerperal sepsis
correctAnswer: Amniotic fluid embolism
explanation: Amniotic fluid embolism typically presents during labour or shortly after delivery with abrupt hypoxia and hypotension, often followed rapidly by disseminated intravascular coagulation. Thromboembolism and high neuraxial block can cause collapse but do not characteristically produce immediate severe consumptive coagulopathy; sepsis usually has an infectious prodrome.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with poorly controlled gestational diabetes presents at 34 weeks with progressive breathlessness and rapid abdominal enlargement. Symphysiofundal height is well above dates, the uterus is tense, fetal parts are difficult to palpate and fetal heart sounds are distant, while there is no vaginal bleeding or uterine tenderness. Which obstetric condition best explains the findings?
options: Undiagnosed multifetal gestation
Fetal macrosomia without excess fluid
Concealed placental abruption
Severe polyhydramnios
correctAnswer: Severe polyhydramnios
explanation: A tense overdistended uterus with difficult fetal palpation and muffled heart sounds is characteristic of marked polyhydramnios, for which diabetes is an important maternal association. Multifetal gestation and macrosomia enlarge the uterus but do not usually obscure fetal parts in this manner, while concealed abruption causes pain, tenderness and maternal compromise.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 22-year-old has oligomenorrhea, acne and the ovarian image shown. TSH and prolactin are normal. Which additional feature would complete Rotterdam criteria if ultrasound is counted?
options: Prolactin excess explaining the menstrual disturbance
High FSH pattern suggesting primary ovarian insufficiency
Clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism
Low gonadotropin pattern suggesting hypothalamic amenorrhoea
correctAnswer: Clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism
explanation: Clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism completes Rotterdam criteria when ultrasound already shows polycystic ovarian morphology and oligo-ovulation is present. PCOS is diagnosed after excluding mimics such as thyroid disease and hyperprolactinemia. Hydronephrosis, CA-125 and beta-hCG are not Rotterdam criteria.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 39-year-old woman has progressively heavy regular menses, pelvic pressure and iron-deficiency anaemia with a firm irregularly enlarged uterus. The displayed ultrasound shows a sharply marginated solid myometrial mass with a whorled echotexture and peripheral vascularity rather than diffuse myometrial heterogeneity. Which pathology is most likely?
options: Focal adenomyoma
Uterine leiomyoma
Uterine leiomyosarcoma
Transient myometrial contraction
correctAnswer: Uterine leiomyoma
explanation: A well-circumscribed whorled myometrial mass in a woman with heavy menstrual bleeding and pressure symptoms is typical of leiomyoma. Adenomyoma is often ill-defined within a heterogeneous myometrium, leiomyosarcoma cannot be diagnosed solely by rapid growth or routine ultrasound, and a contraction is transient on repeat imaging.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman receiving a magnesium-sulfate infusion for eclampsia becomes somnolent, with absent patellar reflexes, a respiratory rate of 8 per minute and urine output of 15 mL per hour. Oxygenation and ventilatory support are initiated because renal magnesium clearance is impaired and respiratory arrest is imminent. Which immediate drug-and-infusion action is required?
options: Stop magnesium and administer intravenous calcium gluconate
Stop magnesium and administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate
Continue magnesium and administer intravenous diazepam
Continue magnesium and administer intravenous furosemide
correctAnswer: Stop magnesium and administer intravenous calcium gluconate
explanation: Loss of deep-tendon reflexes followed by respiratory depression in an oliguric patient indicates magnesium toxicity. The infusion must be stopped and intravenous calcium gluconate given to antagonise neuromuscular and cardiac effects, while airway support and subsequent magnesium elimination are addressed.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: Seven days after an hCG trigger during controlled ovarian stimulation, a woman develops abdominal distension, dyspnoea and reduced urine output. Ultrasonography shows bilaterally enlarged multicystic ovaries with ascites, and laboratory testing reveals haemoconcentration and rising creatinine despite increased total body fluid. Which treatment-related complication best explains this intravascular depletion with third spacing?
options: Adnexal torsion after ovarian stimulation
Ruptured haemorrhagic corpus-luteum cyst
Pelvic infection after oocyte retrieval
Severe ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome
correctAnswer: Severe ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome
explanation: Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome follows stimulation and hCG exposure, with vasoactive permeability causing enlarged ovaries, ascites, haemoconcentration, oliguria and sometimes pleural effusion or thrombosis. Torsion, cyst rupture and infection may cause acute pain but do not produce this characteristic systemic capillary-leak pattern.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 47-year-old woman presents with a newly noticed unilateral solid mass in the Bartholin-gland region, without fever or fluctuation. Because a typical duct cyst is less common at this age and a rare gland carcinoma can mimic benign disease, simple drainage alone would not establish the diagnosis. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Word-catheter drainage with cytology of expressed fluid
Marsupialisation with microbiological sampling of the cavity
Magnetic-resonance characterisation followed by interval examination
Biopsy or excision to obtain histopathology
correctAnswer: Biopsy or excision to obtain histopathology
explanation: A new Bartholin-region mass in a woman older than 40 warrants tissue histopathology to exclude malignancy, particularly when it is solid or atypical. Fluid cytology and microbiology do not assess invasive architecture, while imaging can define extent but cannot replace tissue diagnosis. Word-catheter drainage and marsupialisation are intended for uncomplicated cysts or abscesses rather than an unexplained solid mass.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: An early-pregnancy unit is matching findings with management consequences. Match the following: Column I: 1. Stable selected unruptured ectopic 2. Shock with hemoperitoneum 3. Complete mole after evacuation 4. Rising hCG after molar evacuation Column II: a. Methotrexate may be considered b. Emergency operative management c. Serial beta-hCG surveillance d. Persistent GTN concern Which option gives the correct matching?
explanation: The matched consequences are medical ectopic treatment eligibility, emergency surgery, molar hCG surveillance and concern for persistent trophoblastic disease.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A 27-year-old woman with obesity, oligomenorrhoea and biochemical hyperandrogenism has anovulatory infertility due to PCOS after thyroid disease, hyperprolactinaemia and non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia are excluded. Tubes are patent, semen analysis is normal and lifestyle optimisation has not restored ovulation. Which pharmacologic agent is preferred for first-line ovulation induction?
options: Letrozole
Clomiphene citrate
Metformin alone
Injectable gonadotropins
correctAnswer: Letrozole
explanation: Letrozole is preferred first-line pharmacologic ovulation induction for anovulatory infertility in PCOS when no other infertility factor is present, with better ovulation and live-birth outcomes than clomiphene in many populations. Metformin alone is less effective, while gonadotropins are generally reserved for oral-agent failure with close monitoring.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman previously delivered viable twins in a single pregnancy, both children are alive, and she is now pregnant again with one fetus. A trainee is asked to update her obstetric score and must distinguish the number of pregnancies reaching viability from the number of living children. How should the previous twin delivery contribute to parity and living children?
options: Two parity events and two living children
One parity event and one living child
One parity event and two living children
Two parity events and one living child
correctAnswer: One parity event and two living children
explanation: Parity counts pregnancies that reached the defined viability threshold, irrespective of the number of fetuses in that pregnancy. A single twin pregnancy therefore contributes one parity event, while two surviving twins contribute two living children to the obstetric score.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A 22-week fetal neurosonogram confirms lateral-ventricular atrial widths of 12 mm on two correctly obtained views, with no anomaly yet identified on the limited survey. The parents ask whether an apparently isolated finding can simply be observed, but ventriculomegaly may accompany chromosomal, genomic or congenital-infection disorders. Which evaluation is most appropriate now?
options: Detailed neurosonography, diagnostic genetic testing and targeted infection assessment
Repeat atrial measurement, maternal serum screening and routine growth surveillance
Fetal echocardiography, umbilical-artery Doppler and broad maternal TORCH serology
Immediate fetal MRI, antenatal corticosteroids and planned preterm delivery
correctAnswer: Detailed neurosonography, diagnostic genetic testing and targeted infection assessment
explanation: Confirmed ventriculomegaly requires detailed anatomic and neurosonographic assessment, offering diagnostic genetic testing and targeted evaluation for infections such as CMV and toxoplasmosis, followed by interval imaging. Screening alone is less definitive, broad untargeted TORCH panels are inferior to specific testing, and ventricular size alone does not mandate preterm delivery.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: At full cervical dilatation, vaginal examination and the displayed image confirm a face presentation with the orbital ridges, nose and mouth palpable. The fetal head is fully extended, so the usual occipital flexion point cannot be reached without placing a cup over facial structures. Which statement about vacuum-assisted delivery is correct?
options: Vacuum extraction is permissible when the mentum is anterior
Vacuum extraction is permissible with the cup over the malar region
Vacuum extraction is contraindicated in face presentation
Vacuum extraction is preferred when the mentum is posterior
correctAnswer: Vacuum extraction is contraindicated in face presentation
explanation: Vacuum extraction requires safe cup placement on the flexion point of a flexed vertex and is contraindicated in face presentation because this placement is impossible and facial or ocular injury may result. Mentum position determines the possibility of vaginal birth, but it does not make vacuum application safe.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: After a difficult vaginal delivery, systematic examination under adequate analgesia identifies disruption of the perineal muscles and the anal sphincter complex. Digital rectal examination confirms that the anorectal mucosa is intact and there is no isolated rectal buttonhole injury. What is the overall obstetric perineal-tear classification?
options: Second-degree perineal tear
Third-degree perineal tear
Fourth-degree perineal tear
Rectal buttonhole tear
correctAnswer: Third-degree perineal tear
explanation: Any obstetric tear involving the external or internal anal sphincter is third degree when the anorectal mucosa remains intact. Second-degree injury stops at perineal muscle, fourth-degree injury extends through anorectal mucosa, and a buttonhole tear involves rectal mucosa with an intact sphincter complex.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: At 37 weeks, a singleton fetus remains in transverse lie in a woman with intact membranes, reassuring fetal assessment and no placenta previa, uterine scar or other contraindication to manipulation. She is not in labour, and immediate caesarean capability is available if fetal compromise occurs. Which intervention may be offered to reduce the likelihood of persistent malpresentation at delivery?
options: Internal podalic version before the onset of labour
External cephalic version under appropriate monitoring
Oxytocin induction while the transverse lie persists
Vacuum rotation after spontaneous membrane rupture
correctAnswer: External cephalic version under appropriate monitoring
explanation: External cephalic version may be offered near term for an eligible singleton transverse lie with fetal monitoring and emergency delivery facilities available. Internal podalic version is not routine singleton antenatal management, and induction or operative vaginal delivery is unsafe while a transverse lie persists.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with a mechanical mitral valve has remained on dose-adjusted warfarin during part of pregnancy and is now 36 weeks with a planned delivery. She has no valve thrombosis, but anticoagulation must remain therapeutic while fetal exposure, neuraxial timing and haemorrhage at delivery are controlled by a cardiology-obstetric-anaesthetic team. Which near-delivery strategy is most appropriate?
options: Continue warfarin until spontaneous labour and reverse it only after contractions begin
Reduce warfarin and add low-dose aspirin without using therapeutic heparin
Change to therapeutic heparin with monitoring and a timed pre-delivery interruption
Stop warfarin and use prophylactic-dose heparin until after delivery
correctAnswer: Change to therapeutic heparin with monitoring and a timed pre-delivery interruption
explanation: Near planned delivery, warfarin is replaced by a monitored therapeutic unfractionated-heparin or low-molecular-weight-heparin regimen, which can be stopped at a defined interval to permit safer delivery and anaesthesia. Continuing warfarin into labour risks fetal and maternal bleeding, whereas aspirin or prophylactic heparin alone is inadequate valve protection.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A fetal limb abnormality is detected on the anomaly scan, and karyotype and the remaining detailed anatomy are normal. The displayed defect is irregular and unilateral, with a distal constriction and amputation pattern that does not follow a developmental limb-reduction plane. Which morphologic feature most strongly supports amniotic-band disruption rather than a chromosomal or skeletal syndrome?
options: Symmetric rhizomelic shortening involving all four limbs
Bilateral postaxial polydactyly with associated organ anomalies
Asymmetric constriction across a non-embryologic tissue plane
Matched longitudinal limb defects with a recurrent family pattern
correctAnswer: Asymmetric constriction across a non-embryologic tissue plane
explanation: Amniotic-band sequence is a disruptive process producing asymmetric constriction rings, distal oedema, entrapment or amputations across irregular planes. Symmetric limb shortening, syndromic polydactyly or recurrent patterned longitudinal defects instead suggest genetic, chromosomal or skeletal-development disorders.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A pregnancy has crossed the routine statutory gestational limit for termination, and the case is referred for assessment by the state or union-territory Medical Board under the Indian MTP framework. The board requires specified clinical specialists to evaluate the woman and fetal diagnosis rather than a general administrative panel. Which professional is not listed as a core medical member by virtue of that professional role?
options: Obstetrician-gynaecologist
Legal adviser
Paediatrician
Radiologist or sonologist
correctAnswer: Legal adviser
explanation: The statutory Medical Board includes an obstetrician-gynaecologist, a paediatrician, a radiologist or sonologist and any additional member notified by the government. A legal adviser may assist an institution with process, but that role is not one of the specified core medical-member categories.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A symptomatic 8 cm mature cystic teratoma is shown in a nulliparous 23-year-old. Ovary appears viable. Best surgery?
options: Ovarian cystectomy
Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy
Salpingo-oophorectomy
Observation with interval imaging
correctAnswer: Ovarian cystectomy
explanation: Laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy is preferred when feasible for a symptomatic benign dermoid in a young patient with a viable ovary. Fertility-preserving surgery is the key principle. Radical surgery, methotrexate and endometrial sampling do not match a benign ovarian dermoid.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A fetus has an isolated asymmetric constriction and distal hand defect consistent with amniotic-band sequence, while detailed anatomy, growth and chromosomal testing are otherwise reassuring. The parents are unrelated, there is no family history of limb anomalies and they ask about recurrence in another pregnancy. Which counselling statement is most appropriate?
options: It is usually autosomal dominant, with a 50 percent recurrence risk
It is usually autosomal recessive, with a 25 percent recurrence risk
It is usually sporadic, with no meaningful increase above baseline recurrence risk
It is usually chromosomal, with recurrence determined mainly by maternal age
correctAnswer: It is usually sporadic, with no meaningful increase above baseline recurrence risk
explanation: Isolated amniotic-band sequence is generally a sporadic disruption rather than a Mendelian or chromosomal syndrome, so recurrence risk is usually not increased above the background level. A patterned, symmetric or familial anomaly would justify reconsidering an inherited diagnosis and its specific recurrence risk.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A 35-week woman arrives with antepartum bleeding. Ultrasound report is unavailable and fetal tracing is concerning. Which clinical pattern favors abruption over placenta previa?
options: Painless bright bleeding with soft uterus
Contact bleeding from friable cervix
Painful bleeding with tender hypertonic uterus
Fetal bleeding after membrane rupture
correctAnswer: Painful bleeding with tender hypertonic uterus
explanation: Painful bleeding with a tender hypertonic uterus favors placental abruption. Placenta previa usually presents with painless bleeding and a soft uterus. Cervical and fetal-vessel bleeding patterns do not match the uterine tenderness and fetal compromise described.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: After suction evacuation of a complete hydatidiform mole, a woman begins serial beta-hCG surveillance and wants a reversible contraceptive method. She has no contraindication to combined or progestin-only hormonal contraception, and avoiding a new pregnancy is important so that hCG trends remain interpretable. Which timing plan is most appropriate?
options: Begin hormonal contraception now and defer intrauterine contraception until hCG remission
Defer hormonal contraception until hCG remission and insert an intrauterine device now
Begin hormonal contraception now and insert an intrauterine device during persistent hCG elevation
Defer both hormonal and intrauterine contraception until surveillance is complete
correctAnswer: Begin hormonal contraception now and defer intrauterine contraception until hCG remission
explanation: Reliable contraception should begin during post-molar hCG surveillance, and eligible hormonal methods may be started promptly. Intrauterine contraception is deferred until hCG has returned to normal because persistent trophoblastic disease and uterine bleeding may still require assessment and treatment.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: During vaginal breech birth, the trunk and arms have delivered but the after-coming head remains in the pelvis. The operator supports the fetal body astride the forearm, places two fingers on the maxilla to maintain flexion and applies the other hand over the shoulders and occiput. Which named manoeuvre is being performed?
options: Burns-Marshall manoeuvre
Lovset manoeuvre
Mauriceau-Smellie-Veit manoeuvre
Prague manoeuvre
correctAnswer: Mauriceau-Smellie-Veit manoeuvre
explanation: The Mauriceau-Smellie-Veit manoeuvre maintains flexion of the after-coming head by supporting the maxilla while the fetal body rests on the operator's forearm and the other hand applies shoulder-occipital pressure. Lovset releases extended arms, while Burns-Marshall and Prague use different mechanics for the after-coming head.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 28-year-old woman with regular ovulatory cycles and her healthy partner have had appropriately timed unprotected intercourse for seven months without conception. Neither has a history suggesting tubal disease, endometriosis, gonadotoxic treatment, sexual dysfunction or male-factor infertility, and examination is unremarkable. When should a routine infertility evaluation begin if pregnancy does not occur?
options: After 12 months of trying
After 6 months of trying
After 18 months of trying
After 24 months of trying
correctAnswer: After 12 months of trying
explanation: For a woman younger than 35 with no risk factor requiring earlier assessment, infertility evaluation is generally initiated after 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse. Evaluation begins earlier when age is 35 or more or when a known reproductive risk factor is present.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with seven weeks amenorrhoea, spotting and unilateral pain is stable. Ultrasound shows an extrauterine gestational sac. What is the diagnosis?
options: Threatened intrauterine pregnancy
Corpus luteum cyst
Tubal ectopic pregnancy
Complete abortion
correctAnswer: Tubal ectopic pregnancy
explanation: A definite gestational sac outside the uterine cavity establishes ectopic pregnancy. Stability determines whether selected medical treatment is possible; instability or rupture requires urgent surgery.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A stable patient has an unruptured tubal ectopic. Statements: A. Methotrexate can be considered if criteria are met. B. Hemodynamic instability is a contraindication to medical treatment. C. Reliable follow-up is required. D. Suction evacuation is definitive treatment for all ectopics. Which are correct?
options: A, B and D only
A, C and D only
B, C and D only
A, B and C only
correctAnswer: A, B and C only
explanation: Statements A, B and C are correct. Methotrexate is limited to carefully selected stable, unruptured ectopic pregnancy with reliable follow-up. Haemodynamic instability or significant haemoperitoneum requires urgent operative management; suction evacuation is not definitive treatment for every ectopic pregnancy.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: An amenorrhoea work-up is matching phenotype with likely diagnosis. Match the following: Column I: 1. Absent uterus with normal ovaries 2. 46,XY with absent uterus and breast development 3. High FSH with streak gonads 4. Cyclic pain with hematocolpos Column II: a. MRKH syndrome b. Complete androgen insensitivity c. Turner syndrome/gonadal dysgenesis d. Imperforate hymen Which option gives the correct matching?
explanation: Mullerian agenesis causes an absent uterus with normal ovaries and normal secondary sexual characteristics. Complete androgen insensitivity has a 46,XY karyotype, absent Mullerian structures and breast development with sparse androgen-dependent hair. Gonadal dysgenesis produces streak gonads and hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, while an imperforate hymen obstructs menstrual outflow and causes cyclic pain with haematocolpos.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with infertility and heavy menstrual bleeding has saline-infusion sonography showing a type 0 submucosal leiomyoma that distorts the endometrial cavity. Ovarian reserve and semen analysis are acceptable, and she wishes to preserve fertility while correcting a likely mechanical impediment to implantation. Which intervention is most appropriate?
options: Laparoscopic myomectomy
Uterine-artery embolisation
Long-term GnRH-agonist therapy alone
Hysteroscopic myomectomy
correctAnswer: Hysteroscopic myomectomy
explanation: A type 0 cavity-distorting submucosal fibroid is directly accessible hysteroscopically, and removal can improve bleeding and reproductive outcomes. Laparoscopic myomectomy is suited to selected intramural or subserosal lesions, embolisation is generally avoided when future fertility is a priority, and medical suppression is temporary rather than definitive.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: Thirty minutes after vaginal birth, a woman has ongoing postpartum haemorrhage despite uterine massage, intravenous oxytocin and active resuscitation. The team addresses uterine tone and genital-tract trauma while preparing blood products, and there is no contraindication to an antifibrinolytic. Which tranexamic-acid regimen should be added?
options: Give 1 g intravenously after the first 3 hours and repeat 1 g after another hour
Give 2 g intravenously within 3 hours and omit a repeat dose
Give 1 g intravenously within 3 hours and repeat 1 g after 24 hours
Give 1 g intravenously within 3 hours of birth and repeat 1 g if bleeding persists or restarts
correctAnswer: Give 1 g intravenously within 3 hours of birth and repeat 1 g if bleeding persists or restarts
explanation: Tranexamic acid should be given as early as possible and within 3 hours of birth for clinically diagnosed postpartum haemorrhage, as a 1 g intravenous dose over about 10 minutes. A second 1 g dose may be given if bleeding continues after 30 minutes or restarts within 24 hours; source control and uterotonics continue.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with a previous classical caesarean incision is 38 weeks pregnant, and the current fetus remains breech. She has no contractions or scar tenderness, but an upper-segment uterine scar carries a substantial risk of rupture during labour regardless of whether version could correct presentation. Which delivery plan is safest?
options: External cephalic version followed by planned trial of labour
Spontaneous trial of labour with forceps for the after-coming head
Planned caesarean at term before labour begins
Oxytocin induction after successful external cephalic version
correctAnswer: Planned caesarean at term before labour begins
explanation: A previous classical upper-segment caesarean scar is a contraindication to labour because rupture risk is substantially higher than with a low-transverse scar. Planned repeat caesarean before labour addresses the scar risk; changing breech to cephalic presentation does not make induction or trial of labour acceptable.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A young woman has symptomatic apical uterovaginal prolapse with little cervical elongation and wishes to retain her uterus. During counselling, she asks about a mesh-based abdominal or laparoscopic operation that attaches the cervix and upper vagina to the anterior longitudinal ligament over the sacral promontory. Which procedure is described?
options: Sacrospinous hysteropexy
Sacrohysteropexy
Uterosacral hysteropexy
Manchester-Fothergill operation
correctAnswer: Sacrohysteropexy
explanation: Sacrohysteropexy restores apical support while preserving the uterus by suspending the cervix or uterus to the sacral promontory, usually with mesh. Sacrospinous and uterosacral hysteropexies use vaginal ligamentous attachment points, while Manchester surgery primarily addresses cervical elongation.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A 15-year-old with short stature, webbed neck and primary amenorrhoea has hypergonadotropic hypogonadism and a 45,X cell line, with testing also detecting Y-chromosome material. Cardiac and renal assessment is arranged before hormonal treatment because Turner syndrome requires lifelong multisystem surveillance. Which reproductive-endocrine plan is appropriate?
options: Begin full-dose estrogen and progestogen together and retain Y-bearing gonadal tissue
Use cyclic progestogen alone and remove gonads only after a pelvic mass develops
Begin low-dose estrogen, add cyclic progestogen later and remove Y-bearing gonadal tissue
Begin gonadotropin ovulation induction and retain streak gonads for spontaneous puberty
correctAnswer: Begin low-dose estrogen, add cyclic progestogen later and remove Y-bearing gonadal tissue
explanation: Pubertal induction in Turner syndrome uses gradually escalating estrogen, with cyclic progestogen added after adequate estrogenisation or breakthrough bleeding. Y-chromosome material increases gonadoblastoma risk and warrants prophylactic gonadectomy, while cardiovascular, renal, thyroid, metabolic, hearing and bone health require ongoing surveillance.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 42-year-old woman has progressive dysmenorrhoea, heavy menstrual bleeding and a uniformly enlarged tender uterus. The displayed MRI is reviewed to distinguish diffuse adenomyosis from a circumscribed leiomyoma before discussing medical treatment and uterus-preserving procedures. Which imaging feature most strongly favours adenomyosis?
options: Sharply marginated whorled mass with a peripheral pseudocapsule
Well-circumscribed subserosal mass with bridging uterine vessels
Diffuse junctional-zone thickening with ill-defined myometrial foci
Pedunculated intracavitary mass surrounded by endometrial fluid
correctAnswer: Diffuse junctional-zone thickening with ill-defined myometrial foci
explanation: Adenomyosis typically produces a thickened irregular junctional zone, ill-defined low-signal myometrial change and small high-signal foci from ectopic glands or haemorrhage. Leiomyomas are more often sharply circumscribed whorled masses, whether intramural, subserosal or intracavitary.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A 36-year-old woman has a benign uterine smooth-muscle tumour that enlarged during pregnancy but became smaller after lactation and is expected to regress after menopause. Histology shows intersecting fascicles without atypia, coagulative necrosis or increased mitoses, excluding leiomyosarcoma. Which receptor biology best explains this life-stage-dependent growth pattern?
options: Androgen- and mineralocorticoid-receptor responsiveness
Prolactin- and growth-hormone-receptor responsiveness
FSH- and LH-receptor responsiveness
Estrogen- and progesterone-receptor responsiveness
correctAnswer: Estrogen- and progesterone-receptor responsiveness
explanation: Leiomyomas are hormonally responsive, particularly to estrogen and progesterone, which supports growth during reproductive years and pregnancy and regression after menopause. Their growth is not primarily driven by androgen, prolactin, growth-hormone or gonadotropin receptors.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A 31-year-old woman who desires future pregnancy has cervical adenocarcinoma in situ identified after glandular cytologic abnormality and colposcopic sampling. Because AIS may extend into the endocervical canal and contain skip lesions, an intact specimen is needed to exclude occult invasion and assess both endocervical and ectocervical margins. Which initial procedure best provides this information?
options: Loop excision performed in several separately labelled fragments
Endocervical curettage with multiple directed punch biopsies
Simple hysterectomy followed by retrospective margin assessment
Cold-knife conisation with an intact oriented specimen
correctAnswer: Cold-knife conisation with an intact oriented specimen
explanation: Adenocarcinoma in situ requires diagnostic excision that yields an intact oriented specimen with interpretable ectocervical and endocervical margins and adequate canal depth, commonly cold-knife conisation. Fragmented loop specimens compromise margin interpretation, and curettage plus punch biopsies cannot reliably exclude occult invasion. Hysterectomy should follow appropriate diagnostic excision rather than substitute for it.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A diabetic woman has pruritus and thick discharge. Wet mount image shows fungal elements. She is 12 weeks pregnant. Which is the best treatment choice?
options: Single-dose oral fluconazole
Topical azole for 7 days
Oral metronidazole for 7 days
Oral acyclovir for 5 days
correctAnswer: Topical azole for 7 days
explanation: Vulvovaginal candidiasis in pregnancy is treated with topical azoles, not routine oral fluconazole. Candida vulvovaginitis presents with pruritus, curdy discharge and budding yeast or pseudohyphae. In pregnancy, topical azole therapy is preferred.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: An ovarian mass is being interpreted with age, imaging or histology and tumour-marker implications. Assess the following statements: A. Yolk sac tumour is associated with AFP. B. Granulosa-cell tumour may have estrogenic manifestations and inhibin elevation. C. Krukenberg tumour classically contains metastatic signet-ring cells. D. The most common malignant transformation in a dermoid is adenocarcinoma. Which statements are correct?
options: A, B and C only
A, B and D only
A, C and D only
B, C and D only
correctAnswer: A, B and C only
explanation: A is true: Yolk sac tumour is associated with AFP. B is true: Granulosa-cell tumour may have estrogenic manifestations and inhibin elevation. C is true: Krukenberg tumour classically contains metastatic signet-ring cells. Statement D is false. Squamous cell carcinoma is the most common malignant transformation in a mature cystic teratoma.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A 17-year-old has a unilateral ovarian yolk-sac tumour, markedly elevated AFP, no radiologic metastasis and a grossly normal contralateral ovary and uterus. She desires future fertility, but treatment must still provide oncologic staging and permit postoperative platinum-based chemotherapy and AFP surveillance. Which primary surgical approach is most appropriate?
options: Ovarian cystectomy with fertility-sparing surgical staging
Unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with fertility-sparing staging
Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with uterine-preserving staging
Unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with routine contralateral wedge biopsy
correctAnswer: Unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with fertility-sparing staging
explanation: Malignant ovarian germ-cell tumours in young patients are generally managed with fertility-sparing unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and appropriate staging while retaining a normal uterus and opposite ovary. Cystectomy alone risks residual disease, bilateral removal is unnecessary when the opposite ovary is normal, and routine wedge biopsy of a grossly normal contralateral ovary can damage fertility without improving staging. AFP guides postoperative surveillance and chemotherapy response.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with previous severe early-onset pre-eclampsia and fetal growth restriction books at 11 weeks in a new pregnancy. Blood pressure is currently normal, there is no aspirin allergy or bleeding disorder, and the team plans placental-disease prevention before abnormal spiral-artery remodelling is established. Which medication should be started in the late first trimester?
options: Daily magnesium sulphate
Daily prophylactic warfarin
Daily low-dose aspirin
Daily methylergometrine
correctAnswer: Daily low-dose aspirin
explanation: A history of early-onset pre-eclampsia is a high-risk indication for low-dose aspirin, begun around 12 weeks and ideally before 16 weeks unless contraindicated. Magnesium sulphate prevents or treats eclamptic seizures, warfarin is not routine placental prophylaxis, and methylergometrine is a postpartum uterotonic that can worsen hypertension.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: After the fetal head delivers, it retracts tightly against the perineum and gentle axial traction does not release the shoulders. The team calls for help, asks the mother to stop pushing and begins a structured shoulder-dystocia sequence to reduce hypoxia and brachial-plexus injury. Which manoeuvre must be avoided because it can worsen anterior-shoulder impaction?
options: McRoberts positioning
Fundal pressure
Suprapubic pressure
Posterior-arm delivery
correctAnswer: Fundal pressure
explanation: Fundal pressure drives the impacted anterior shoulder more firmly against the pubic symphysis and increases maternal and fetal injury risk. McRoberts positioning and suprapubic pressure are first-line measures, followed when necessary by internal rotation or delivery of the posterior arm.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman becomes pregnant with a copper intrauterine device in situ, and transvaginal ultrasonography confirms a viable intrauterine pregnancy with no ectopic component. She wishes to continue the pregnancy, and speculum examination shows the device strings protruding through the cervix. Which next step offers the best balance of pregnancy continuation and complication reduction?
options: Gently remove the device by its visible strings after counselling
Leave the device in place because removal always carries greater risk
Perform blind intrauterine retrieval even if the strings retract
Recommend termination solely because conception occurred with the device
correctAnswer: Gently remove the device by its visible strings after counselling
explanation: When an intrauterine pregnancy is continued and IUCD strings are visible, gentle early removal is recommended because retention carries higher risks of miscarriage, septic complications and preterm birth. Blind intrauterine instrumentation is avoided when strings are not accessible, and the device alone does not mandate termination.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: A 54-year-old woman has weight loss, early satiety and dyspepsia, and imaging shows bilateral solid ovarian masses. Biopsy demonstrates mucin-filled signet-ring cells with a stromal reaction, indicating metastatic Krukenberg tumours rather than a primary ovarian epithelial neoplasm. Which investigation should be prioritised to locate the classic primary site?
options: Colonoscopy with ileal intubation
Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy
Diagnostic mammography and breast ultrasonography
Colposcopy with directed cervical biopsy
correctAnswer: Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy
explanation: Krukenberg tumour classically represents ovarian metastasis from a gastric signet-ring adenocarcinoma, making upper gastrointestinal endoscopy a priority in this clinical setting. Colorectal, breast and cervical primaries can involve ovaries but are less consistent with dyspepsia and the classic histology described.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Questions_Audited_Consolidated.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_500_Final_Answers_Explanations_Audited_Consolidated.pdf

subject: ObGyn
question: A 17-year-old presents with a rapidly enlarging unilateral ovarian mass and abdominal pain, while serum AFP is markedly elevated and beta-hCG is normal. Histology shows reticular architecture, eosinophilic hyaline globules and perivascular structures resembling primitive glomeruli. Which malignant ovarian germ-cell tumour is identified by this combination?
options: Dysgerminoma
Embryonal carcinoma
Yolk-sac tumour
Immature teratoma
correctAnswer: Yolk-sac tumour
explanation: Schiller-Duval bodies, hyaline globules and marked AFP elevation are characteristic of a yolk-sac tumour. Dysgerminoma is more closely associated with LDH, embryonal carcinoma may raise both AFP and beta-hCG, and immature teratoma is graded by immature neuroepithelial tissue.
source: Question PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Questions.pdf; explanations PDF: OBGYN_All_700_Corrected_Answers_Explanations.pdf; StudyDoc audit pass: 2026-06-30

subject: ObGyn
question: At 31 weeks, a woman with severe polyhydramnios has marked abdominal discomfort, orthopnoea and difficulty eating, with an amniotic-fluid index of 38 cm. There is no labour, fetal testing is reassuring and evaluation for diabetes, alloimmunisation, infection and structural anomalies is underway. Which intervention can provide short-term maternal symptom relief while the cause and ongoing pregnancy plan are addressed?
options: A short indomethacin course prescribed solely to reduce fluid volume
Ultrasound-guided therapeutic amnioreduction for maternal symptom relief
Maternal loop-diuretic therapy with serial fluid-volume assessment
Preterm caesarean delivery after antenatal corticosteroid administration
correctAnswer: Ultrasound-guided therapeutic amnioreduction for maternal symptom relief
explanation: Therapeutic amnioreduction can provide short-term relief of marked maternal respiratory and abdominal symptoms from severe polyhydramnios while diagnostic evaluation and surveillance continue. Indomethacin is not recommended solely to lower amniotic-fluid volume because of fetal ductal and renal effects, maternal diuretics do not correct the underlying fluid physiology, and reassuring fetal status with no labour does not justify preterm caesarean delivery.
