images.credit: Image credit: Nevit Dilmen ( talk ) · CC BY-SA 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A first-trimester twin pregnancy has the lambda sign shown in the displayed ultrasound. Both fetuses are structurally normal and growth is concordant. The patient asks whether she needs the same two-weekly ultrasound from 16 weeks used to screen uncomplicated monochorionic twins for TTTS. No other maternal or fetal complication is present. Which surveillance principle is correct? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Begin two-weekly TTTS surveillance from 16 weeks
Use the less intensive uncomplicated dichorionic-twin schedule unless complications arise
Manage as a singleton until labour
Plan fetal laser ablation prophylactically
correctAnswer: Use the less intensive uncomplicated dichorionic-twin schedule unless complications arise
explanation: The lambda sign identifies dichorionic twins, who do not share placental vascular anastomoses and therefore do not require the monochorionic TTTS schedule. They still need twin-specific growth surveillance, intensified if discordance or other complications develop.

images.caption: Ultrasound Scan ND 034.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Nevit Dilmen ( talk ) · CC BY-SA 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A patient with monozygotic twins asks why some identical twins share a placenta while others do not. The displayed four-row illustration links progressively later embryonic division to chorionicity and amnionicity. The row with a single shared placenta but two separate amniotic sacs corresponds to division after chorion formation but before amnion formation. This arrangement is the classic substrate for twin-twin transfusion syndrome. Which configuration is represented by that row? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Dichorionic diamniotic twins
Monochorionic monoamniotic twins
Monochorionic diamniotic twins
Dizygotic twins with fused placentas
correctAnswer: Monochorionic diamniotic twins
explanation: Monochorionic diamniotic twins share one chorion and placenta but occupy separate amniotic sacs, typically after division around days 4-8. Shared placental vascular anastomoses create TTTS risk, so ultrasound surveillance generally begins every two weeks from 16 weeks.

images.caption: Placentation.svg
images.credit: Image credit: Kevin Dufendach · CC BY 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: The displayed placentation illustration includes a row in which two fetuses share both a single placenta and a single amniotic cavity without a dividing membrane. Ultrasound later confirms two independent fetal heart rates but intertwined umbilical cords. There is no TTTS fluid discordance because there are not separate sacs in which to measure deepest pockets. The pregnancy is being counselled at a tertiary fetal-medicine centre. Which complication is particularly characteristic of this configuration? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Twin-twin transfusion defined by oligohydramnios in one separate sac
Vasa previa from a bilobed singleton placenta
Shoulder dystocia caused by fetal macrosomia
Umbilical-cord entanglement in monochorionic monoamniotic twins
correctAnswer: Umbilical-cord entanglement in monochorionic monoamniotic twins
explanation: Monochorionic monoamniotic twins share one amniotic cavity, so cord entanglement is a central risk and surveillance and delivery require specialist planning. Classic TTTS staging depends on separate amniotic sacs and is principally a monochorionic diamniotic complication.

images.caption: Placentation.svg
images.credit: Image credit: Kevin Dufendach · CC BY 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 38-year-old woman has heavy regular bleeding, pressure symptoms and iron-deficiency anaemia. Endometrial sampling is benign, but ultrasound incompletely defines the relationship of a large uterine mass to the cavity. The displayed sagittal and axial MRI panels use source-native red arrows to show a well-circumscribed myometrial lesion projecting into and markedly distorting the endometrial cavity. She desires future fertility. Which lesion is demonstrated? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Submucosal leiomyoma
Endometrial polyp
Cervical carcinoma
Ovarian fibroma
correctAnswer: Submucosal leiomyoma
explanation: A well-defined myometrial mass bulging into the endometrial cavity is a submucosal leiomyoma. A polyp arises from endometrium and lacks a large myometrial component, while cervical carcinoma and ovarian fibroma arise elsewhere.

images.caption: When Hysteroscopy Isn't an Option: Laparoscopic Management of a Symptomatic Submucosal Fibroid. - MRI images of the fibroids; the red arrow depicts the submucosal fibroid.
images.credit: Image credit: Elboghdady MM, Salih S, Al Hamoud AM, Abdalbari H, Hosni W. · CC BY

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with severe heavy menstrual bleeding has the large cavity-distorting fibroid shown by the red arrows in the displayed MRI. The lesion has a substantial intramural component and is too large for safe complete single-stage hysteroscopic removal at the local unit. Medical treatment has failed, fertility preservation is desired and malignancy is not suspected. The team can perform minimally invasive myomectomy. Which principle best guides treatment? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Perform blind curettage as definitive fibroid treatment
Choose myomectomy route by size and intramural extension; a laparoscopic approach may be required
Use endometrial ablation despite future fertility plans
Perform routine hysterectomy because all submucosal fibroids are malignant
correctAnswer: Choose myomectomy route by size and intramural extension; a laparoscopic approach may be required
explanation: Small predominantly intracavitary fibroids are often resected hysteroscopically, but a large lesion with major intramural extension may require laparoscopic or open myomectomy by an experienced surgeon. Curettage is ineffective, ablation compromises fertility, and benign leiomyoma does not mandate hysterectomy.

images.caption: When Hysteroscopy Isn't an Option: Laparoscopic Management of a Symptomatic Submucosal Fibroid. - MRI images of the fibroids; the red arrow depicts the submucosal fibroid.
images.credit: Image credit: Elboghdady MM, Salih S, Al Hamoud AM, Abdalbari H, Hosni W. · CC BY

subject: ObGyn
question: A 32-year-old woman with severe dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia and infertility has the multifocal disease distribution shown in the displayed labeled laparoscopic composite. Some panels are superficial, but the lowest row includes deep disease near structures that may involve bowel or ureter. Ultrasound has not mapped the full deep compartment, and she wants fertility-preserving surgery. Proceeding from the visible superficial implants alone could leave occult organ involvement or cause injury. Which preparation is most appropriate before definitive excision? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Ablate only the easiest superficial lesions without further mapping
Perform radical hysterectomy solely from the visual stage
Map deep disease and plan multidisciplinary surgery when organs are involved
Begin empiric antibiotics as definitive treatment
correctAnswer: Map deep disease and plan multidisciplinary surgery when organs are involved
explanation: The composite demonstrates disease across superficial, ovarian and deep compartments. Suspected deep endometriosis near bowel, bladder or ureter should be mapped with expert imaging and managed by an appropriately multidisciplinary team while incorporating fertility goals.

images.caption: Endometriosis types laparoscopy.webp
images.credit: Image credit: Leibetseder et al. · CC BY 4.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A patient with pain and infertility has the range of endometriotic lesions shown in the displayed laparoscopic composite. One panel suggests deep disease near the rectovaginal compartment, while other panels show superficial and ovarian involvement. Symptoms, fertility goals and organ involvement must guide treatment rather than the visual stage alone. She has no bowel obstruction or ureteric compromise and wants pregnancy soon. Which operative principle is most appropriate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Perform radical hysterectomy for every visible implant
Ablate lesions blindly without mapping adjacent organs
Ignore deep lesions because superficial implants are also present
Individualize fertility-preserving excision and use a multidisciplinary team for deep disease
correctAnswer: Individualize fertility-preserving excision and use a multidisciplinary team for deep disease
explanation: Endometriosis surgery should be individualized to symptoms, fertility goals and lesion location. Deep disease near bowel, bladder or ureter may require multidisciplinary expertise, while radical surgery is not automatic in a patient desiring fertility.

images.caption: Endometriosis types laparoscopy.webp
images.credit: Image credit: Leibetseder et al. · CC BY 4.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 66-year-old multiparous woman reports a vaginal bulge, urinary urgency and occasional stool leakage. Neurologic examination is normal, and urinalysis is negative. The displayed NIH infographic separates three pelvic-floor disorder groups and shows one panel in which pelvic organs descend toward the vaginal opening. Her symptoms span more than one compartment rather than representing an isolated bladder infection. Which underlying mechanism best unifies the illustrated disorders? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Failure of pelvic-floor muscle and connective-tissue support
Excess ovarian estrogen causing organ enlargement
Isolated renal tubular dysfunction
Congenital absence of the Mullerian ducts
correctAnswer: Failure of pelvic-floor muscle and connective-tissue support
explanation: Weakness or injury of pelvic-floor muscles and connective tissue can produce urinary and bowel-control symptoms and pelvic-organ prolapse, exactly the groups shown in the infographic. The alternatives do not explain descent of supported pelvic organs.

images.caption: Pelvic Floor Infographic (32919738987).jpg
images.credit: Image credit: NIH Image Gallery from Bethesda, Maryland, USA · Public domain

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 72-year-old woman recognizes her vaginal-bulge symptoms in the prolapse panel of the displayed infographic. Examination confirms symptomatic anterior and apical prolapse, but there is no hydronephrosis, ulceration or urinary retention. She wants a low-burden reversible option and is not ready for surgery. Pelvic-floor muscle training alone has provided incomplete relief. Which next treatment is appropriate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Mandate hysterectomy for every symptomatic prolapse
Offer a properly fitted vaginal pessary
Prescribe antibiotics as definitive prolapse treatment
Perform endometrial ablation to restore pelvic support
correctAnswer: Offer a properly fitted vaginal pessary
explanation: A vaginal pessary is an effective, reversible conservative treatment for symptomatic pelvic-organ prolapse and should be fitted with follow-up for discharge, erosion and ongoing need. Surgery is individualized to symptoms, compartment defects, health and goals rather than mandatory.

images.caption: Pelvic Floor Infographic (32919738987).jpg
images.credit: Image credit: NIH Image Gallery from Bethesda, Maryland, USA · Public domain

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A primiparous woman develops a widened genital hiatus and pelvic heaviness after a prolonged second stage and forceps birth. The displayed inferior-view anatomical chart labels the paired levator sheet around the urogenital and anal openings. MRI later confirms unilateral avulsion of that sheet from its pubic attachment. She has no neurologic disease or pelvic mass. Which long-term disorder is most directly promoted by this injury? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Endometrial carcinoma from unopposed estrogen
Bilateral hydrosalpinx from distal tubal occlusion
Pelvic-organ prolapse from loss of levator support
Primary ovarian insufficiency from follicle depletion
correctAnswer: Pelvic-organ prolapse from loss of levator support
explanation: Levator ani avulsion widens the hiatus and weakens dynamic pelvic support, increasing pelvic-organ-prolapse risk and sometimes urinary symptoms. It does not cause endometrial cancer, distal tubal occlusion or ovarian follicle depletion.

images.caption: MAC TAV 0035 Tavola parietale pavimento pelvico femminile umano.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Unknown Unknown · Public domain

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 48-year-old woman leaks small amounts of urine with coughing and exercise. Urinalysis is normal, postvoid residual is low and examination shows no major prolapse. The displayed pelvic-floor chart identifies the muscle group she must learn to contract without substituting gluteal or abdominal muscles. She has not yet received structured conservative treatment. Which first-line intervention is recommended? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Immediate long-term indwelling catheter
Antimuscarinic monotherapy for stress leakage
Routine hysterectomy with no continence procedure
Supervised pelvic-floor muscle training for at least three months
correctAnswer: Supervised pelvic-floor muscle training for at least three months
explanation: Supervised pelvic-floor muscle training is first-line treatment for stress or mixed urinary incontinence and should include assessment of a correct contraction and an adequate programme. Antimuscarinics treat urgency, while catheters and hysterectomy do not correct uncomplicated stress leakage.

images.caption: MAC TAV 0035 Tavola parietale pavimento pelvico femminile umano.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Unknown Unknown · Public domain

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman has continuous urinary leakage after pelvic trauma and difficult vaginal instrumentation. Dye testing confirms a lower urinary-tract fistula, but its relationship to the continence mechanism is uncertain. In the displayed perineal photograph, a Foley catheter remains within the urethra for only about 2 cm before emerging through a large defect that extends toward the bladder neck. The ureteric orifices have not yet been mapped. How should this fistula be classified? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Complex vesicovaginal fistula involving the bladder neck or proximal urethra
Simple high vesicovaginal fistula remote from the continence mechanism
Isolated rectovaginal fistula
Ureterovaginal fistula with an intact bladder outlet
correctAnswer: Complex vesicovaginal fistula involving the bladder neck or proximal urethra
explanation: The catheter's early abnormal exit and extension to the bladder neck show a complex outlet-involving urinary fistula. This anatomy threatens continence and requires more extensive evaluation than a simple fistula away from the urethra and ureters.

images.caption: Addressing complicated vesicovaginal fistula post-intercourse in MRKH: the critical imperative for comprehensive sex education and awareness - Showing Foleys catheter inside the urethra only for the initial 2 cm beyond which it came out through the complicated vesicovaginal fistula extending till the bladder neck.
images.credit: Image credit: NCBI · CC BY

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A patient has the complex bladder-neck fistula demonstrated by the catheter path in the displayed photograph. She has no sepsis or obstructed kidney, and inflamed tissues are being allowed to recover. Cystoscopy and upper-tract imaging will define ureteric involvement before repair. A simple office closure would risk outlet stenosis, persistent leakage and incontinence. Which management principle is most appropriate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Treat with pelvic-floor exercises alone
Plan specialist reconstructive repair with bladder-outlet and continence assessment
Perform nephrectomy without upper-tract disease
Close the visible skin opening without mapping the tract
correctAnswer: Plan specialist reconstructive repair with bladder-outlet and continence assessment
explanation: A complex fistula involving the bladder neck or proximal urethra needs experienced reconstructive planning, mapping of ureters and outlet, tension-free layered closure and postoperative drainage, with continence consequences discussed. Exercises or superficial closure cannot repair the tract.

images.caption: Addressing complicated vesicovaginal fistula post-intercourse in MRKH: the critical imperative for comprehensive sex education and awareness - Showing Foleys catheter inside the urethra only for the initial 2 cm beyond which it came out through the complicated vesicovaginal fistula extending till the bladder neck.
images.credit: Image credit: NCBI · CC BY

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 37-year-old nonpregnant woman has a positive high-risk HPV test but no visible cervical lesion. The cytology category is withheld so that management is not chosen from HPV status alone. The displayed ThinPrep image shows a tight cluster of small cells with scant cytoplasm, hyperchromatic crowded nuclei and coarse chromatin beside a much larger mature squamous cell. Koilocytic cytoplasmic halos are not the dominant feature. Which cytology category is represented? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Negative for intraepithelial lesion
Low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion
High-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion
Atypical glandular cells
correctAnswer: High-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion
explanation: Small dysplastic squamous cells with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, hyperchromasia and coarse chromatin are characteristic of HSIL. LSIL more often shows koilocytosis and greater cytoplasmic maturation, while glandular abnormalities have different architecture.

images.caption: High-Grade SIL, ThinPrep (4306966632).jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Ed Uthman from Houston, TX, USA · CC BY 2.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A nonpregnant 32-year-old woman has the HSIL cytomorphology shown in the displayed ThinPrep field. HPV16 testing is positive, she has not been screened for nine years and the cervix has no gross cancer. The immediate CIN3-plus risk is therefore high, but future fertility matters and occult invasion must still be considered. She is able to attend follow-up. Which management discussion is appropriate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Return to routine five-year screening
Perform cryotherapy without evaluating the transformation zone
Give HPV vaccine as sole treatment for the current lesion
Discuss risk-based colposcopy or eligible expedited excisional treatment
correctAnswer: Discuss risk-based colposcopy or eligible expedited excisional treatment
explanation: HSIL requires risk-based evaluation. Colposcopy with biopsy is appropriate, and expedited excisional treatment may be offered to eligible nonpregnant adults when immediate CIN3-plus risk meets the guideline threshold, especially HPV16-positive or rarely screened HSIL; vaccination does not treat established disease.

images.caption: High-Grade SIL, ThinPrep (4306966632).jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Ed Uthman from Houston, TX, USA · CC BY 2.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 56-year-old woman is referred for colposcopy after persistent high-risk HPV and HSIL cytology. Her anatomy matches the rightmost panel of the displayed labeled transformation-zone diagram, with the squamocolumnar junction extending beyond full view into the canal. No invasive lesion is visible on the ectocervix, but the unseen endocervical epithelium cannot be declared normal. She is not pregnant and has no cervical stenosis. Which additional assessment is most appropriate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Obtain endocervical sampling as part of risk-based evaluation
Declare the examination negative without sampling
Perform blind ablation of the unseen canal
Return directly to routine five-year screening
correctAnswer: Obtain endocervical sampling as part of risk-based evaluation
explanation: When the squamocolumnar junction is not fully visualized, endocervical disease must be assessed according to cytology, HPV risk and guideline pathways. Blind ablation and routine screening are inappropriate in a patient with HSIL and an incompletely assessed endocervical component.

images.caption: Transformation zone types.png
images.credit: Image credit: Mikael Häggström , M.D. Author info - Reusing images - Conflicts of interest: None Mikael Häggström , M.D. · CC0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A postmenopausal woman with HSIL cytology has the type 3 anatomy shown in the displayed diagram. No invasive cancer is visible, but the upper limit of the transformation zone cannot be inspected and ablation would leave unassessed epithelium in the canal. Endocervical sampling is concerning for high-grade disease. She is not pregnant and has no contraindication to excision. Which next step is most appropriate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Perform blind ablative treatment despite an unseen upper limit
Perform a diagnostic excisional procedure that samples the endocervical transformation zone
Return to routine screening without tissue diagnosis
Treat with antibiotics and repeat cytology after five years
correctAnswer: Perform a diagnostic excisional procedure that samples the endocervical transformation zone
explanation: High-grade findings with a type 3 transformation zone require tissue assessment of the unseen endocervical component; a diagnostic excision is appropriate when risk and sampling support it. Ablation is unsuitable when the lesion or transformation-zone upper limit is not fully visible.

images.caption: Transformation zone types.png
images.credit: Image credit: Mikael Häggström , M.D. Author info - Reusing images - Conflicts of interest: None Mikael Häggström , M.D. · CC0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A trainee reviews the displayed superior-view pelvic-inlet drawing before assessing labour arrest. The source-native lines show the transverse, oblique and anteroposterior dimensions of the superior pelvic aperture. The fetal biparietal diameter must engage through this plane, but the clinically limiting anteroposterior space is not measured directly on vaginal examination. Which inlet dimension is most relevant to that anteroposterior constraint? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Interspinous diameter
Intertuberous diameter
Obstetric conjugate
Bitrochanteric diameter
correctAnswer: Obstetric conjugate
explanation: The obstetric conjugate is the shortest anteroposterior distance from the sacral promontory to the posterior pubic symphysis and governs inlet passage. Interspinous and intertuberous diameters describe the midpelvis and outlet, while bitrochanteric diameter is fetal.

images.caption: Gray238.png
images.credit: Image credit: Henry Vandyke Carter · Public domain

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A nulliparous woman in spontaneous labour has a pelvis whose inlet axes are illustrated in the displayed drawing. At 6 cm dilatation the occiput is anterior, contractions have only recently become adequate and maternal and fetal status are reassuring. A short woman is not automatically diagnosed with cephalopelvic disproportion, and routine radiographic pelvimetry does not reliably predict vaginal birth. No arrest criterion has yet been met. Which management is most appropriate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Perform immediate caesarean solely from maternal height
Order routine X-ray pelvimetry before every vaginal birth
Attempt operative vaginal delivery before full dilatation and low station
Continue labour assessment and diagnose arrest only by accepted clinical criteria
correctAnswer: Continue labour assessment and diagnose arrest only by accepted clinical criteria
explanation: Cephalopelvic disproportion is a functional clinical diagnosis based on progress with adequate contractions, fetal position and station, not a single anthropometric impression. Without accepted arrest criteria or compromise, continued assessment is appropriate.

images.caption: Gray238.png
images.credit: Image credit: Henry Vandyke Carter · Public domain

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 30-year-old woman has infertility and hypomenorrhoea after postpartum curettage, and the displayed labeled HSG documents corporal intrauterine adhesions. Ovarian reserve, thyroid function and prolactin are normal, and both tubes opacify. Hysteroscopic restoration of the cavity is successful and she later conceives. The prior endometrial trauma may leave abnormal decidualization even after the cavity is reopened. Which obstetric risk deserves specific antenatal counselling? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Placenta accreta spectrum and other abnormal placentation
Neural-tube defect from folate antagonism
Congenital diaphragmatic hernia
Fetal hemolysis from Rh alloimmunization
correctAnswer: Placenta accreta spectrum and other abnormal placentation
explanation: Severe intrauterine adhesions and prior endometrial trauma are associated with miscarriage, preterm birth and abnormal placentation including placenta accreta spectrum. The displayed cavity disease does not independently cause the unrelated fetal or alloimmune conditions listed.

images.caption: HSG Ashermans syndrome.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Floranerolia Floranerolia · CC BY-SA 4.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with infertility has the intrauterine adhesion pattern shown in the displayed HSG. There is no active pelvic infection, and she desires pregnancy. Blind curettage could worsen the cavity, while HSG defines the defect but cannot safely divide it. The team wants direct visualization and controlled restoration of the cavity. Which treatment is preferred? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Repeat blind sharp curettage
Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis under direct vision
Bilateral salpingectomy
Endometrial ablation
correctAnswer: Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis under direct vision
explanation: Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis is the treatment of choice for symptomatic intrauterine adhesions because it restores the cavity under direct vision. Postoperative strategies to reduce re-adhesion and assess cavity healing are individualized; blind curettage and ablation can worsen fertility.

images.caption: HSG Ashermans syndrome.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Floranerolia Floranerolia · CC BY-SA 4.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 29-year-old woman with unexplained infertility undergoes a monitored ovulation-induction cycle. Estradiol is appropriate, the endometrium is trilaminar and there are no symptoms of ovarian hyperstimulation. The displayed colour-Doppler scan shows a single anechoic follicle measuring about 20 x 24 mm with peripheral vascularity and a visible cumulus oophorus. No additional intermediate or mature follicles are present. What does the image indicate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Ovarian endometrioma
Tubo-ovarian abscess
A mature dominant preovulatory follicle
Polycystic ovary with no dominant follicle
correctAnswer: A mature dominant preovulatory follicle
explanation: A smooth anechoic follicle around 20-24 mm with a cumulus oophorus is a mature dominant follicle ready for ovulation or protocol-based triggering. Endometrioma has ground-glass echoes, abscess is complex and inflammatory, and polycystic morphology alone lacks this mature dominant appearance.

images.caption: Mature ovarian follicle.png
images.credit: Image credit: Luis F. Goncalves · CC BY-SA 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A patient in a monitored non-IVF cycle has the single mature follicle shown in the displayed ultrasound. There are no additional follicles that would create an unacceptable high-order multiple-pregnancy risk. The endometrium is developed, she has no OHSS symptoms and the semen sample is suitable for insemination. The clinic follows a validated hCG-trigger protocol. Which next step is reasonable? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Cancel solely because one mature follicle is present
Double the gonadotropin dose after maturity
Drain the follicle without an IVF retrieval plan
Administer the planned ovulation trigger and time intercourse or insemination
correctAnswer: Administer the planned ovulation trigger and time intercourse or insemination
explanation: A single mature follicle with an appropriate endometrium supports protocol-based trigger and timed intercourse or intrauterine insemination. Cancellation is used for excessive response or other safety concerns, while escalating stimulation or unplanned aspiration is inappropriate.

images.caption: Mature ovarian follicle.png
images.credit: Image credit: Luis F. Goncalves · CC BY-SA 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 24-year-old woman previously had a definite six-week intrauterine pregnancy documented on ultrasound. She now presents after heavy bleeding and passage of tissue, but is haemodynamically stable and pain has subsided. The displayed transvaginal scan shows no gestational sac and a thin endometrial cavity measured at approximately 7.1 mm. There is no adnexal mass or significant free fluid. Which diagnosis best fits the image and sequence? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Complete miscarriage
Missed miscarriage with retained embryo
Pregnancy of unknown location
Incomplete miscarriage with substantial retained tissue
correctAnswer: Complete miscarriage
explanation: After a previously confirmed intrauterine pregnancy, passage of tissue followed by an empty cavity and thin endometrium supports complete miscarriage. Pregnancy of unknown location does not apply because location was established, and the image lacks a persistent sac or substantial retained products.

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A stable patient has the post-bleeding ultrasound appearance shown in the displayed image after a previously confirmed intrauterine pregnancy. Bleeding is now light, temperature and haemoglobin are normal and there is no sonographic retained gestational sac. She is RhD negative and appropriate anti-D considerations are addressed according to local guidance and gestation. She asks whether curettage is mandatory to make the scan normal. Which plan is most appropriate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Perform emergency curettage solely for a 7.1-mm endometrium
Provide expectant follow-up and safety-netting without routine uterine evacuation
Give methotrexate for presumed ectopic pregnancy
Perform laparotomy despite clinical stability
correctAnswer: Provide expectant follow-up and safety-netting without routine uterine evacuation
explanation: A clinically well patient with a complete miscarriage and no substantial retained tissue does not require routine evacuation. Follow-up confirms symptom resolution, with urgent reassessment for heavy bleeding, fever, worsening pain or persistent pregnancy testing.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 22-week anomaly scan is performed after elevated maternal serum AFP. The abdominal wall is intact and amniotic fluid is normal. The displayed single axial fetal-head ultrasound frame shows bilateral frontal-bone scalloping that gives the skull a lemon-shaped contour. No spinal image or posterior-fossa panel is included in the source. Which fetal abnormality is this cranial sign most strongly associated with? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Isolated fetal cleft lip and palate
Congenital left diaphragmatic hernia
Open spina bifida with Chiari II changes
Fetal duodenal atresia with polyhydramnios
correctAnswer: Open spina bifida with Chiari II changes
explanation: The lemon sign is an indirect cranial marker strongly associated with open spina bifida and Chiari II malformation in the second trimester. The static frame raises suspicion but must be followed by direct spinal and posterior-fossa assessment.

images.caption: Lemon and banana sign for neural tube defect Dr Wolfgang Moroder.theora.ogv

subject: ObGyn
question: A fetus at 23 weeks has the lemon-sign contour shown in the displayed axial head frame. Chromosomal screening was low risk, but this does not exclude associated structural or genetic abnormalities. Because the displayed source does not show the spine, cerebellum or ventricles, the diagnosis and prognosis cannot be completed from that frame alone. The parents request counselling about fetal intervention. Which next assessment is most appropriate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: No further imaging because the lemon sign proves an isolated defect
Maternal brain MRI instead of fetal assessment
Immediate delivery before multidisciplinary review
Detailed neurosonography with direct spinal survey and offered diagnostic genetic testing
correctAnswer: Detailed neurosonography with direct spinal survey and offered diagnostic genetic testing
explanation: A lemon sign requires targeted fetal neurosonography, direct evaluation of the entire spine and posterior fossa, a full anomaly survey and an offer of diagnostic genetic testing. Fetal MRI and fetal-surgery eligibility assessment may follow based on complete findings.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 33-year-old woman at 32 weeks presents with painless bright-red bleeding and the placental relationship shown in the abnormal panel of the displayed labeled illustration. The uterus is soft, fetal status is reassuring and there is no trauma. Placental location has not yet been confirmed with current imaging, and labour status is uncertain. A speculum and ultrasound are available immediately. Which bedside action should be avoided at this stage? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Digital vaginal examination before placenta previa is excluded
Maternal haemodynamic assessment and intravenous access
Continuous assessment of fetal status
Transvaginal ultrasound by an experienced operator
correctAnswer: Digital vaginal examination before placenta previa is excluded
explanation: When placental tissue may cover the internal os, digital vaginal examination can provoke severe haemorrhage and must be deferred until previa is excluded. Maternal-fetal assessment and transvaginal ultrasound are appropriate and safe when performed correctly.

images.caption: 2906 Placenta Previa-02.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: OpenStax College · CC BY 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman at 36 weeks has two self-limited painless bleeds and the placental relationship shown in the abnormal panel of the displayed illustration. Transvaginal ultrasound confirms persistent placental tissue covering the internal os. She is stable, haemoglobin has been optimized and fetal testing is reassuring. Blood products and experienced staff are available. Which delivery plan is appropriate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Routine induction with amniotomy
Planned caesarean birth with haemorrhage preparation
Digital cervical examination to reconfirm location
Delay until post-term spontaneous labour
correctAnswer: Planned caesarean birth with haemorrhage preparation
explanation: Persistent placenta previa covering the os precludes vaginal birth and requires planned caesarean delivery with timing individualized to bleeding and current guidance. Digital examination before previa is excluded can provoke haemorrhage.

images.caption: 2906 Placenta Previa-02.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: OpenStax College · CC BY 3.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A singleton IVF pregnancy has a bilobed placenta and the fetal-vessel arrangements shown in the displayed labeled diagram. Screening ultrasound suggests vessels near the cervix, but maternal cervical flow and a fetal vessel require different management. Placental tissue itself does not cover the os, and there has been no bleeding. The team must verify both the spatial relationship and whose circulation is being sampled. Which test best confirms the dangerous arrangement? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Digital examination to directly palpate the suspected vessel
Maternal serum CA-125 testing for placental disease
Transvaginal Doppler confirming fetal-rate vessels over the os
Abdominal pelvic radiography without vascular Doppler assessment
correctAnswer: Transvaginal Doppler confirming fetal-rate vessels over the os
explanation: Vasa previa is confirmed by transvaginal colour Doppler showing vessels traversing the membranes over or near the internal os and pulsed Doppler demonstrating a waveform at the fetal heart rate. Digital examination risks rupture and the other tests cannot identify fetal membranous vessels.

images.caption: Vasa Previa - English Translation.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Sigrid de Rooij · CC BY-SA 4.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A pregnancy at 32 weeks has the fetal-vessel arrangement shown in the displayed vasa-previa diagram, confirmed by transvaginal colour Doppler with a fetal-rate waveform. There has been no bleeding or membrane rupture, and fetal testing is normal. The patient lives far from emergency operative and neonatal care. The team discusses corticosteroids, possible admission and avoiding labour or membrane rupture. Which birth plan is preferred? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Await spontaneous rupture of membranes
Induce labour at term with amniotomy
Attempt operative vaginal birth before rupture
Planned prelabour caesarean birth with individualized late-preterm timing
correctAnswer: Planned prelabour caesarean birth with individualized late-preterm timing
explanation: Prenatally diagnosed vasa previa is managed to avoid labour and membrane rupture, usually with planned late-preterm caesarean individualized to symptoms, cervical status and access to emergency care. Admission and corticosteroid timing are also individualized.

images.caption: Vasa Previa - English Translation.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Sigrid de Rooij · CC BY-SA 4.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: Immediately after placental delivery, a woman develops severe pelvic pain, postpartum haemorrhage and shock out of proportion to the visible blood loss. The uterine fundus cannot be palpated abdominally. The displayed perineal clinical photograph shows a smooth hemorrhagic uterine mass protruding through the introitus with a central fundal depression. A prolapsed fibroid would not make the abdominal fundus disappear acutely. Which diagnosis is shown? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Acute complete uterine inversion
Uterine atony without inversion
Cervical prolapse alone
Prolapsed submucosal fibroid
correctAnswer: Acute complete uterine inversion
explanation: A fundus turned inside out and protruding through the vagina, together with an absent abdominal fundus and disproportionate shock, identifies complete uterine inversion. Atony leaves the uterus in its normal position, and a fibroid does not invert the entire fundus.

images.caption: InvertedUterus.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Lawrence Mbuagbaw, Patrick Mbah Okwen · CC BY 2.5

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A patient has major postpartum bleeding and the complete inversion shown in the displayed photograph. Resuscitation, anaesthesia, blood products and theatre staff are mobilized immediately. The placenta remains partly attached, and giving uterotonics before correction could tighten the cervix around the inverted fundus. The operator is ready to act while resuscitation continues. What is the immediate priority? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Apply additional cord traction
Prompt manual uterine replacement before uterotonics
Wait for spontaneous replacement after fluids
Give a uterotonic before attempting replacement
correctAnswer: Prompt manual uterine replacement before uterotonics
explanation: Immediate manual replacement is the priority alongside haemorrhage resuscitation; anaesthetic relaxation or operative methods may be required if it fails. Uterotonics are given after successful replacement to maintain tone, and traction can worsen inversion and bleeding.

images.caption: InvertedUterus.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Lawrence Mbuagbaw, Patrick Mbah Okwen · CC BY 2.5

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: Ten days after vaginal birth, a woman has persistent moderate bleeding and a subinvoluted uterus. Temperature is 37.5 degrees C, haemoglobin is 9.6 g/dL and she is haemodynamically stable. The displayed single grayscale sagittal ultrasound uses a source-native arrow to mark focal echogenic tissue within the endometrial cavity. No colour-Doppler information is present in this source, so an arteriovenous lesion cannot be assessed from flow. Which diagnosis is most likely in this context? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Normal postpartum lochia only
Ovarian torsion
Retained products of conception
Complete uterine inversion
correctAnswer: Retained products of conception
explanation: Persistent postpartum bleeding, subinvolution and focal echogenic intracavitary tissue support retained products of conception. Doppler can refine the differential from clot or vascular lesions, but the displayed grayscale finding and clinical picture are not normal lochia.

images.caption: An Unusual Case of Rising Serum βhCG in the Setting of Retained Products of Conception: A Case Report. - Ultrasound appearance of retained products of conception

subject: ObGyn
question: A postpartum patient has ongoing bleeding, falling haemoglobin and uterine tenderness. Broad-spectrum antibiotics are started because endometritis is suspected. The displayed grayscale ultrasound shows the focal intracavitary retained tissue marked by the arrow, while separate Doppler assessment excludes a myometrial arteriovenous malformation. Bleeding continues despite uterotonics. Which next treatment is most appropriate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Continue antibiotics alone despite ongoing postpartum blood loss
Perform blind endometrial ablation of the postpartum cavity
Perform uterine-artery embolization without addressing retained tissue
Ultrasound-guided uterine evacuation with haemorrhage preparation
correctAnswer: Ultrasound-guided uterine evacuation with haemorrhage preparation
explanation: Symptomatic retained products with ongoing bleeding and possible infection generally require uterine evacuation, preferably with imaging guidance and haemorrhage readiness. Observation is for selected stable low-burden cases, and ablation or ovarian surgery is inappropriate.

images.caption: An Unusual Case of Rising Serum βhCG in the Setting of Retained Products of Conception: A Case Report. - Ultrasound appearance of retained products of conception
images.credit: Image credit: Sklar K. · CC BY

topic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy
subject: ObGyn
question: At 28 weeks, a woman develops unilateral left-leg swelling and groin pain. Oxygen saturation is normal and she has no chest symptoms. The displayed source-native A/B ultrasound panels label the artery and vein before and during probe pressure; arrows mark echogenic material within the vein. During compression the vein fails to collapse. Which diagnosis is established? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Proximal deep-vein thrombosis
Physiologic pregnancy oedema
Superficial thrombophlebitis only
Ruptured Baker cyst
correctAnswer: Proximal deep-vein thrombosis
explanation: Failure of a proximal vein to compress around intraluminal thrombus is diagnostic of DVT. Physiologic oedema is usually bilateral, and the alternatives do not produce this noncompressible common-femoral-vein appearance.

topic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy
subject: ObGyn
question: A 30-week pregnant patient has the noncompressible proximal vein shown in the displayed ultrasound. She is stable, has no pulmonary symptoms or active bleeding, and platelet count and renal function are normal. Delivery is not imminent, and she asks whether an oral factor-Xa inhibitor would be easier. A peripartum neuraxial plan will be needed later. Which initial treatment is preferred? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Use a direct oral anticoagulant continuously until labour
Therapeutic low-molecular-weight heparin with an individualized peripartum plan
Use low-dose aspirin as sole anticoagulant therapy
Withhold anticoagulation solely because the patient is pregnant
correctAnswer: Therapeutic low-molecular-weight heparin with an individualized peripartum plan
explanation: Therapeutic LMWH is standard treatment for pregnancy-associated DVT because it does not cross the placenta. DOACs are not recommended in pregnancy, and aspirin is inadequate; anticoagulation duration and timing around birth and neuraxial anaesthesia require specialist planning.

The arrow indicates an intraluminal echogenic thrombus. A denotes the artery, and V denotes the vein. In panel A (before co

subject: ObGyn
question: A 19-year-old woman presents with sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain and vomiting. Pregnancy testing is negative, temperature is normal and a benign adnexal cyst was known previously. The displayed anatomical illustration shows the ovary rotated on its vascular pedicle, with venous congestion and a dark hemorrhagic enlarged ovary. Analgesia improves pain only briefly. Which diagnosis is represented? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Uncomplicated ovulatory pain
Tubo-ovarian abscess
Adnexal torsion
Degenerating uterine fibroid
correctAnswer: Adnexal torsion
explanation: Twisting of the adnexal vascular pedicle causes venous and lymphatic obstruction, ovarian oedema and hemorrhage, producing the illustrated torsion pattern and acute pain with vomiting. The alternatives do not twist the adnexal pedicle.

images.caption: Ovarian torsion with hemmorhage svg hariadhi.svg
images.credit: Image credit: Hariadhi · CC BY-SA 4.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A reproductive-age patient has the twisted hemorrhagic adnexa shown in the displayed illustration. Ultrasound still detects some arterial flow, which does not exclude torsion because the ovary has dual blood supply. Symptoms began six hours ago, fertility preservation is important and there is no evidence of invasive malignancy. The ovary may look dark at surgery from venous congestion. Which management is appropriate? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Delay until all arterial flow disappears
Perform routine oophorectomy solely because the ovary is dark
Treat with antibiotics and repeat imaging in one week
Urgent laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian conservation when feasible
correctAnswer: Urgent laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian conservation when feasible
explanation: Suspected torsion requires urgent surgery. Detorsion with ovarian preservation is recommended in reproductive-age patients even when the ovary appears dark because function often recovers; residual flow must not delay intervention.

images.caption: Ovarian torsion with hemmorhage svg hariadhi.svg
images.credit: Image credit: Hariadhi · CC BY-SA 4.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 31-year-old woman has severe dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia and two years of infertility. Pregnancy testing is negative, and she has no fever or leukocytosis. The displayed transvaginal ultrasound shows a large smooth-walled ovarian cyst with homogeneous low-level ground-glass internal echoes. No fat-fluid level, calcified tooth or reticular fibrin strands are visible. Which lesion is most likely? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Ovarian endometrioma
Hemorrhagic corpus-luteum cyst
Mature cystic teratoma
Tubo-ovarian abscess
correctAnswer: Ovarian endometrioma
explanation: A smooth ovarian cyst with homogeneous ground-glass echoes is characteristic of an endometrioma. Hemorrhagic cysts often show a lacy fibrin pattern, dermoids have fat-related echogenic features, and abscesses occur with infection and complex inflammatory morphology.

images.caption: Endometrioma ground glass appearance.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Keckstein et al · CC BY 4.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 34-year-old woman with infertility has the endometrioma appearance shown in the displayed ultrasound. The cyst is 3.5 cm, contains no suspicious solid nodule and causes manageable pain. Ovarian reserve is already low, but oocyte retrieval remains technically accessible. She plans IVF and asks for routine drainage to improve live birth. Which plan is best? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Aspirate every endometrioma before stimulation
Do not perform routine pre-IVF surgery solely to improve outcome
Perform bilateral oophorectomy before IVF
Use prolonged antibiotics before ovarian stimulation
correctAnswer: Do not perform routine pre-IVF surgery solely to improve outcome
explanation: Routine surgery or aspiration of an uncomplicated endometrioma before IVF has not consistently improved live birth and can reduce ovarian reserve. Intervention is individualized for severe pain, suspicious features, size, access to follicles or complications.

images.caption: Endometrioma ground glass appearance.jpg
images.credit: Image credit: Keckstein et al · CC BY 4.0

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A 33-year-old woman with prior pelvic inflammatory disease has two years of infertility. Ovulation and semen analysis are normal, and the uterine cavity is regular. The displayed source-native HSG panels show both tubes distended at their ampullary ends, no free fimbrial spill on the initial image and persistent retained contrast on the delayed image. Arrows mark the dilated distal segments. Which abnormality is present? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Bilateral proximal tubal spasm
Normal bilateral tubal patency
Bilateral hydrosalpinges with distal occlusion
Unicornuate uterus
correctAnswer: Bilateral hydrosalpinges with distal occlusion
explanation: Dilated clubbed distal tubes with absent free spill and delayed retained contrast identify bilateral hydrosalpinx from distal occlusion. Proximal spasm prevents early tubal filling but does not create these enlarged ampullary segments.

images.caption: Evaluation of pregnancy outcomes using a novel hysterosalpingography scoring system for tubal patency. - Two‑sided hydrosalpinx on HSG. (a) Initial image demonstrates failure of contrast to spill through the bilateral fimbrial ostia with marked dilatation of the ampullary segments (wh
images.credit: Image credit: Yang H, Xu Y, Cheng S, Zhang H, Cheng J, Gao F. · CC BY

source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman preparing for IVF has the communicating bilateral hydrosalpinges shown in the displayed initial and delayed HSG panels. Ovarian reserve is acceptable, there is no acute infection and ultrasound confirms persistent tubal fluid. She asks whether the tubes can be ignored because fertilization will occur in the laboratory. The fluid can reflux into the uterine cavity and reduce implantation. Which step improves the reproductive pathway? Interpret the displayed image directly.
options: Proceed directly to embryo transfer without addressing the tubes
Repeat antibiotics until established hydrosalpinges disappear
Perform endometrial ablation before stimulation
Laparoscopic salpingectomy or proximal tubal occlusion before IVF
correctAnswer: Laparoscopic salpingectomy or proximal tubal occlusion before IVF
explanation: Communicating hydrosalpinx fluid reduces implantation and live-birth rates. Salpingectomy or proximal occlusion before IVF improves outcomes when feasible; antibiotics do not reverse chronic distal damage, and ablation destroys the implantation site.

images.caption: Evaluation of pregnancy outcomes using a novel hysterosalpingography scoring system for tubal patency. - Two‑sided hydrosalpinx on HSG. (a) Initial image demonstrates failure of contrast to spill through the bilateral fimbrial ostia with marked dilatation of the ampullary segments (wh

topic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy
subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with type 1 diabetes is planning pregnancy and uses a continuous glucose monitor. Her HbA1c is 7.8%, retinal review is overdue and an ACE inhibitor is still on her medication list. She has frequent nocturnal hypoglycaemia when trying to tighten control. The team wants safer preconception optimization rather than a single rigid glucose target. Which plan is most appropriate?
options: Stop fetotoxic medication, review complications and individualize tighter CGM-guided control
Continue the ACE inhibitor until fetal cardiac activity is seen
Aim for complete normoglycaemia despite recurrent severe hypoglycaemia
Replace insulin with an oral drug before conception
correctAnswer: Stop fetotoxic medication, review complications and individualize tighter CGM-guided control
explanation: Preconception care should stop ACE inhibitors and other fetotoxic drugs, prescribe folate according to local guidance, review retinal and renal disease, and improve glycaemia without provoking severe hypoglycaemia. CGM helps time-in-range management, but insulin remains essential for type 1 diabetes.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

topic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy
subject: ObGyn
question: A 37-year-old with pregestational diabetes has a normally grown fetus at 37 weeks. Glucose control has become erratic despite optimized insulin, and antenatal testing has intermittent abnormalities that recover with observation. There is no preeclampsia, but the cervix is favorable and neonatal support is available. She asks whether every diabetic pregnancy must continue to 40 weeks. Which principle is correct?
options: Deliver every diabetic pregnancy at 34 weeks
Individualize delivery timing by control, complications and fetal surveillance rather than wait automatically
Continue every uncomplicated case beyond 41 weeks
Use HbA1c alone to choose the exact day of birth
correctAnswer: Individualize delivery timing by control, complications and fetal surveillance rather than wait automatically
explanation: Timing for pregestational diabetes is individualized by glycaemic control, vascular disease, fetal growth and surveillance. Well-controlled uncomplicated cases can approach term, whereas poor control or maternal-fetal complications justify earlier delivery; route is determined by obstetric factors, not diabetes alone.

topic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy
subject: ObGyn
question: At eight weeks, a woman with known hypothyroidism reports that she continued her prepregnancy levothyroxine dose. TSH is 8.2 mIU/L with low free T4, adherence is good and iron tablets are taken at the same time each morning. She has no pituitary disease or hyperemesis. Fetal neurodevelopment depends on prompt maternal replacement in early gestation. Which action is best?
options: Stop levothyroxine because pregnancy lowers TSH
Start methimazole to prevent fetal thyroid stimulation
Increase levothyroxine promptly and separate it from iron, then recheck TSH
Defer treatment until the anatomy scan
correctAnswer: Increase levothyroxine promptly and separate it from iron, then recheck TSH
explanation: Established hypothyroidism usually requires an early levothyroxine dose increase, with trimester-appropriate TSH monitoring about every four weeks initially. Iron and calcium reduce absorption and should be separated; untreated overt hypothyroidism increases maternal and fetal risk.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.
subtopic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy

topic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy
subject: ObGyn
question: A woman at nine weeks has weight loss, tremor and a diffuse goitre. Free T4 is clearly elevated, TSH is suppressed and TSH-receptor antibodies are positive. She has no thyroid storm, hepatic disease or compressive symptoms. Antithyroid treatment is necessary, but fetal and maternal drug toxicities differ by trimester. Which regimen is preferred now?
options: Use radioactive iodine during the first trimester
Give high-dose levothyroxine with methimazole block-and-replace
Avoid all treatment until postpartum
Use propylthiouracil in the first trimester, then consider switching to methimazole
correctAnswer: Use propylthiouracil in the first trimester, then consider switching to methimazole
explanation: PTU is preferred in the first trimester to reduce methimazole embryopathy risk; after organogenesis, switching to methimazole reduces prolonged PTU hepatotoxicity. The lowest dose controlling maternal free T4 is used, and radioactive iodine and routine block-and-replace are contraindicated.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

topic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy
subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with generalized epilepsy wants to conceive and has been seizure-free for two years on valproate monotherapy. She previously relapsed when medication was abruptly stopped. She is taking no folate and believes all antiseizure medicines are equally teratogenic. Pregnancy should be planned with both seizure control and fetal risk in mind. Which approach is best?
options: Obtain specialist preconception review to replace valproate if feasible and avoid abrupt withdrawal
Stop valproate immediately without an alternative
Continue valproate automatically because seizure freedom eliminates fetal risk
Add a second antiseizure drug solely to lower valproate exposure
correctAnswer: Obtain specialist preconception review to replace valproate if feasible and avoid abrupt withdrawal
explanation: Valproate has major dose-related teratogenic and neurodevelopmental risks and should be avoided in people who may become pregnant when an effective safer alternative is feasible. Changes require specialist planning before conception because abrupt withdrawal can provoke dangerous seizures; folate and contraceptive counselling are also needed.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.
subtopic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy

topic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy
subject: ObGyn
question: At 24 weeks, a woman taking lamotrigine has two breakthrough tonic-clonic seizures. Adherence is confirmed, sodium and glucose are normal and there is no preeclampsia or infection. Her prepregnancy trough level had been documented, but the current concentration is substantially lower. Physiologic clearance of some antiseizure medicines increases during pregnancy. Which management is appropriate?
options: Add valproate without reviewing lamotrigine exposure
Adjust lamotrigine using clinical response and levels, with a postpartum dose-reduction plan
Stop all medication until fetal maturity
Treat only individual seizures and ignore maintenance concentration
correctAnswer: Adjust lamotrigine using clinical response and levels, with a postpartum dose-reduction plan
explanation: Lamotrigine clearance commonly rises during pregnancy, reducing concentration and seizure protection. Specialist-guided dose adjustment toward the individual's effective baseline, followed by prompt postpartum down-titration as clearance normalizes, balances maternal seizure control and toxicity risk.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.
subtopic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy

topic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy
subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with a mechanical mitral valve is six weeks pregnant. She has normal ventricular function, no prior valve thrombosis and is taking warfarin with a low daily dose. She understands that maternal valve protection and embryopathy risks differ among warfarin, dose-adjusted LMWH and unfractionated heparin. The local unit can perform reliable anti-Xa monitoring. Which counselling is most accurate?
options: Stop anticoagulation because pregnancy is hypercoagulable
Use a prophylactic LMWH dose without anti-Xa monitoring
Choose an individualized specialist anticoagulation strategy with strict monitoring throughout pregnancy
Replace anticoagulation with low-dose aspirin alone
correctAnswer: Choose an individualized specialist anticoagulation strategy with strict monitoring throughout pregnancy
explanation: Mechanical valves require uninterrupted therapeutic anticoagulation planned by a cardio-obstetric team. Options vary by valve, warfarin dose and patient priorities, but LMWH must be dose-adjusted with peak and often trough anti-Xa monitoring; prophylactic dosing or aspirin alone risks fatal valve thrombosis.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.
subtopic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy

topic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy
subject: ObGyn
question: A pregnant woman with severe mitral stenosis develops pulmonary oedema and rapid atrial fibrillation at 30 weeks. Oxygen, cautious diuresis and rate control are initiated, and fetal status is assessed. She remains symptomatic despite optimized medical therapy, but there is no left-atrial thrombus or more than mild regurgitation. The multidisciplinary team considers intervention before delivery. Which option is preferred when anatomy is suitable?
options: Routine exercise training until term
Immediate mechanical valve replacement in every case
Stop diuresis because it reduces placental perfusion
Percutaneous balloon mitral valvotomy at an expert centre
correctAnswer: Percutaneous balloon mitral valvotomy at an expert centre
explanation: Severe symptomatic mitral stenosis refractory to medical therapy can be treated with percutaneous balloon valvotomy during pregnancy when valve anatomy is suitable and expertise is available. It avoids the substantial fetal risk of cardiopulmonary bypass and addresses the obstructive lesion.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.
subtopic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy

topic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy
subject: ObGyn
question: A 36-year-old undergoes emergency caesarean birth after prolonged labour. BMI is 42 kg/m2, she has preeclampsia and mobility will be limited, but there is no bleeding or thrombocytopenia. She has never had VTE and no thrombophilia is known. Mechanical methods are applied while an individualized postpartum risk assessment is completed. Which prophylaxis principle is correct?
options: Give postpartum LMWH for a risk-based duration once haemostasis and neuraxial timing permit
Avoid pharmacologic prophylaxis because she has no previous VTE
Use a direct oral anticoagulant during breastfeeding as first choice
Give one aspirin dose instead of mechanical and heparin measures
correctAnswer: Give postpartum LMWH for a risk-based duration once haemostasis and neuraxial timing permit
explanation: Multiple postpartum risk factors—emergency caesarean, obesity, preeclampsia and immobility—support LMWH prophylaxis for a guideline-determined duration after haemostasis is secure. Timing must respect neuraxial catheter removal; DOACs are not first-line obstetric prophylaxis.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.
subtopic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy

topic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy
subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with a previous unprovoked proximal DVT presents at ten weeks. She is not anticoagulated currently, thrombophilia testing is negative and there is no active bleeding. Her previous event was not related to major surgery or a transient immobilizing injury. Pregnancy and puerperium substantially increase recurrence risk. Which prevention strategy is most appropriate?
options: Use postpartum prophylaxis only because pregnancy risk begins after delivery
Begin antenatal LMWH prophylaxis and continue through six weeks postpartum
Give aspirin alone throughout pregnancy
Wait for leg symptoms before starting anticoagulation
correctAnswer: Begin antenatal LMWH prophylaxis and continue through six weeks postpartum
explanation: A prior unprovoked or estrogen-related VTE generally warrants antenatal LMWH prophylaxis plus six weeks of postpartum prophylaxis, individualized by weight and specialist assessment. Surveillance alone is reserved for selected lower-risk provoked histories, not an unprovoked proximal DVT.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.
subtopic: Medical disorders and drugs in pregnancy

subject: ObGyn
question: At 31 weeks, a primigravida has blood pressures of 170/116 and 168/114 mmHg fifteen minutes apart with severe headache. Oxygen saturation is normal, there is no pulmonary oedema and fetal status is reassuring. Intravenous access has not yet been secured despite repeated attempts. Magnesium sulfate is being prepared but will not lower the pressure sufficiently. Which immediate regimen is appropriate?
options: Extended-release nifedipine with review the next morning
Oral methyldopa as sole emergency treatment
Immediate-release oral nifedipine with protocolized reassessment and escalation
Magnesium sulfate alone until a seizure occurs
correctAnswer: Immediate-release oral nifedipine with protocolized reassessment and escalation
explanation: Persistent acute severe hypertension requires treatment within 30-60 minutes to reduce maternal stroke risk. Immediate-release oral nifedipine is a first-line option when IV access is unavailable, with rapid reassessment; labetalol or hydralazine are common IV alternatives.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman at 34 weeks has persistent blood pressure of 164/112 mmHg despite an initial intravenous labetalol dose. Pulse is 58/min, asthma is severe and there is no heart failure. Platelets, creatinine and transaminases are pending, while seizure prophylaxis is considered separately. Which acute antihypertensive choice is reasonable now?
options: Repeat escalating labetalol despite bradycardia and severe asthma
Sublingual immediate-release nifedipine bitten and swallowed
Delay all treatment until laboratory results return
Intravenous hydralazine with close pressure reassessment
correctAnswer: Intravenous hydralazine with close pressure reassessment
explanation: IV hydralazine is an accepted first-line alternative when labetalol is undesirable because of bradycardia or severe asthma. Blood pressure must be reassessed frequently to avoid both ongoing severe hypertension and excessive hypotension; laboratory evaluation must not delay emergency control.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A woman with severe preeclampsia receives magnesium sulfate. Four hours later she is drowsy, respiratory rate is 8/min, patellar reflexes are absent and urine output has fallen to 15 mL/hour. Oxygen is applied and the infusion is stopped while the magnesium concentration is sent. The clinical syndrome should be treated before the result returns. Which antidote is indicated?
options: Slow intravenous calcium gluconate
Intravenous sodium bicarbonate
Intramuscular vitamin K
Oral potassium chloride
correctAnswer: Slow intravenous calcium gluconate
explanation: Respiratory depression, absent reflexes and oliguria indicate magnesium toxicity. Stop magnesium, support ventilation and give slow IV calcium gluconate; renal impairment reduces magnesium clearance, and severe toxicity may require critical care and dialysis.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A patient with eclampsia has a recurrent generalized seizure ten minutes after completing the standard magnesium loading dose. Airway, oxygenation and glucose are assessed, and the fetal trace is recovering. The maintenance infusion is running, reflexes are present and respiration is normal. There is no evidence of magnesium toxicity. What is the preferred immediate anticonvulsant action?
options: Stop magnesium and use phenytoin first-line
Give an additional intravenous magnesium sulfate bolus
Proceed directly to caesarean during the seizure
Give calcium gluconate despite normal reflexes
correctAnswer: Give an additional intravenous magnesium sulfate bolus
explanation: A recurrent eclamptic seizure without toxicity is treated with an additional magnesium bolus while airway and maternal stabilization continue. Magnesium is superior to phenytoin for eclampsia; delivery follows stabilization and is based on obstetric circumstances, not performed during uncontrolled convulsion.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: At 35+2 weeks, a woman has preeclampsia with persistent severe hypertension and platelets of 84,000/microlitre. Intravenous antihypertensive treatment has controlled the pressure, magnesium is running and fetal status is reassuring. One dose of betamethasone was given two hours ago. Completing the course would require delaying indicated birth. Which plan follows current guidance?
options: Continue expectant management until 37 weeks
Delay exactly forty-eight hours for both steroid doses
Deliver after stabilization without delaying solely to complete corticosteroids
Perform caesarean because preeclampsia always precludes induction
correctAnswer: Deliver after stabilization without delaying solely to complete corticosteroids
explanation: Preeclampsia with severe features at or beyond 34 weeks warrants delivery after maternal stabilization, and birth should not be postponed solely for corticosteroid completion. Route depends on fetal presentation, cervix and urgency; severe disease alone does not mandate caesarean.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A primigravida at 32 weeks has preeclampsia without severe blood pressure, symptoms or laboratory abnormalities. Fetal growth, fluid and Dopplers are normal, and reliable close surveillance is available. She asks why immediate preterm delivery is not automatically required. Which strategy is most appropriate while no severe feature develops?
options: Routine delivery at 32 weeks
Continue pregnancy beyond 41 weeks
Use bed rest alone without maternal laboratory or fetal surveillance
Expectant surveillance with planned delivery at 37 weeks
correctAnswer: Expectant surveillance with planned delivery at 37 weeks
explanation: Preeclampsia without severe features before 37 weeks can be managed expectantly with frequent maternal symptom, blood-pressure and laboratory review plus fetal surveillance. Delivery is recommended at 37 weeks or earlier if severe features or fetal compromise appear.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A nulliparous 39-year-old with chronic hypertension and a previous pregnancy complicated by fetal growth restriction presents at 11 weeks. Renal function and platelets are normal, and there is no aspirin allergy or active bleeding. She asks whether calcium alone replaces antiplatelet prevention. Several independent factors place her at high risk for preeclampsia. Which prophylaxis is indicated?
options: Start low-dose aspirin between 12 and 28 weeks, ideally before 16 weeks
Start full-dose aspirin only after 34 weeks
Withhold aspirin unless proteinuria develops
Use clopidogrel as routine first-line prevention
correctAnswer: Start low-dose aspirin between 12 and 28 weeks, ideally before 16 weeks
explanation: Low-dose aspirin is recommended for patients with one high-risk factor or an appropriate combination of moderate-risk factors, begun after 12 weeks and ideally before 16 weeks, then continued according to guideline. It prevents rather than treats established severe disease.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: A healthy 24-year-old multipara presents at 18 weeks. She has no previous hypertensive disorder, diabetes, renal or autoimmune disease, multifetal pregnancy, obesity or family history of preeclampsia. Blood pressure is normal, and the fetus is appropriately grown. She requests aspirin because a friend was prescribed it. Which counselling is best?
options: Prescribe high-dose aspirin to every pregnant person
Do not prescribe routinely when validated high- or multiple moderate-risk criteria are absent
Begin aspirin only during labour
Substitute prophylactic LMWH for aspirin in all low-risk pregnancies
correctAnswer: Do not prescribe routinely when validated high- or multiple moderate-risk criteria are absent
explanation: Aspirin prophylaxis is targeted to patients meeting established risk criteria; evidence does not support universal high-dose aspirin or LMWH for an otherwise low-risk pregnancy. Risk assessment should be revisited if new conditions arise.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: Five days after an uncomplicated term birth, a woman develops severe headache and visual scintillation. Blood pressure is 172/112 mmHg, urine protein is present and she had normal antenatal pressures. Neurologic examination shows no focal deficit, but postpartum stroke and cerebral venous thrombosis remain in the differential. Severe postpartum hypertension still requires immediate obstetric treatment. Which regimen should be initiated?
options: Reassurance because delivery cures all preeclampsia immediately
Oral methyldopa with review in one week
Urgent antihypertensive therapy plus magnesium sulfate for postpartum preeclampsia with severe features
Aspirin alone without neurologic evaluation
correctAnswer: Urgent antihypertensive therapy plus magnesium sulfate for postpartum preeclampsia with severe features
explanation: Preeclampsia and eclampsia can first present postpartum. Persistent severe hypertension requires rapid treatment, and new neurologic symptoms support magnesium prophylaxis while secondary causes and intracranial complications are assessed.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: Two days postpartum, a woman has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure and blood pressure of 168/110 mmHg. Glucose is normal, there is no prior epilepsy and the uterus is well contracted. Airway support is provided, magnesium sulfate is loaded and severe pressure is treated. Focal deficits persist after the postictal period. What additional action is necessary?
options: Attribute every postpartum seizure to eclampsia without imaging
Stop magnesium because focal signs exclude eclampsia
Discharge once the blood pressure first falls below severe range
Obtain urgent neuroimaging while continuing eclampsia treatment
correctAnswer: Obtain urgent neuroimaging while continuing eclampsia treatment
explanation: Eclampsia treatment must not preclude investigation of atypical features such as persistent focal deficit, prolonged coma or refractory seizures. Urgent neuroimaging evaluates haemorrhage, infarction, venous thrombosis or PRES while magnesium and blood-pressure control continue.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: ObGyn
question: Fifteen minutes after vaginal birth, calibrated loss is 330 mL and bleeding continues. The uterus is boggy, pulse is 108/min and blood pressure is 98/58 mmHg, while the placenta appears complete. A second intravenous line is being placed and help is present.
